question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will establish a redress scheme for women who have been harmed by transvaginal mesh procedures.
answer_text: I am increasingly mindful that those who have been harmed by both Pelvic Mesh and Sodium Valproate have had to wait too long for a response to the Hughes Report’s recommendations on Financial Redress. As you may be aware, my Department made a short statement to coincide with the recent second anniversary of the publication of the Hughes Report in light of this.As medical devices and medicines are matters that remain reserved to Westminster, the three Devolved Administrations have been unable to move forward on this while awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health & Social Care. Regrettably, a UK Government decision on redress has still not been taken.Departmental officials continue to meet on a Four Nations basis, and this was supplemented by a Four Nations Ministerial Meeting on 26 January that focused on this important issue. Along with my colleagues from Scotland & Wales, I pressed Dr Zubir Ahmed MP as the UK Minister responsible for Patient Safety for movement on Financial Redress. I fully acknowledge the recent comments from Professor Hughes and others regarding the absence of a timeline for progressing her Report’s recommendations including those on redress and I will continue to urge the UK Government to provide more clarity regarding its position.Thank you for your continued interest in this important matter. It is paramount that collectively we remain focused on supporting the affected community, and that we remain committed to working together with our UK Government partners towards a positive UK-wide resolution.

answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust provide transvaginal mesh removal surgery and surgeons are able to undertake complete and full mesh excision in complex cases with input from a range of Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDTs) including urology and plastic consultants.Significant work has taken place to improve the patient pathway into the Regional Gynaecology Removal of MESH service in the Belfast Trust. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how clawback funds recovered from community pharmacies are distributed back into the health budget; and (ii) the purposes for which they are applied.
answer_text: The Department has a statutory duty to ensure fair, accurate and prompt payment for HSC commissioned pharmaceutical services and the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff sets out the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by community pharmacies.As part of the community pharmacy reimbursement arrangements, discount clawback refers to a mechanism where the HSC reduces the amount it reimburses pharmacies for prescriptions, based on the assumption that pharmacies receive discount from their suppliers. It aims to control the profits or ‘margin’ that pharmacies make on HSC funded medicines. It is however important to note that this clawback mechanism does not create a separate funding source to the HSC – it is effectively ‘deducted at source’, with a net monthly payment issued to community pharmacies under the remuneration and reimbursement arrangements for medicines supplied by them. It is therefore not recovered from community pharmacies under a separate transaction and distributed back into the health budget.In 2025/26 the guaranteed level of margin (retained purchase profit) for community pharmacy is £34m, an increase from £31.5m in 2024/25. In recent years, the actual annual margin achieved by community pharmacies in Northern Ireland has significantly exceeded the guaranteed level.Importantly, unlike other UK regions, any excess in margin is not recouped.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of meetings held with local universities to discuss resource requirements for the training of professional behaviour analysts; and (ii) the number of bursaries created for individuals wishing to study Applied Behaviour Analysis at those universities, in the last two years.
answer_text: Universities are autonomous institutions and are responsible for determining their own academic provision, including decisions relating to course content, capacity, and associated resource requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of multidisciplinary teams, or equivalent professional teams working in the field of autism, currently operating in each Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the number of posts for behaviour analysts, trained to recognised professional standards, that have been established within each of those teams; and (iii) the number of behaviour analysts registered with the Society for Behaviour Analysis employed within health and social care, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust has two specialist multidisciplinary teams focusing on autism, one in children’s services and one within adult services. Across all Trusts, individuals with autism may also be supported by a wide range of other services depending on their individual needs and presentation.No Trust has established dedicated Behaviour Analyst posts within their specialist autism teams. Trusts deliver autism services in line with NICE guidance, which does not identify Behaviour Analysts as core autism team members. Behavioural assessment and intervention needs are met by Practitioner Psychologists, Behaviour Therapists, and other clinicians with relevant skills. Some staff may hold training in Behaviour Analysis as part of CPD, but this is not a post requirement.All Trusts report that they do not hold records of the number of staff registered with the Society for Behaviour Analysis as this is not a regulatory body.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment undertaken of the condition and suitability of existing supported living accommodation sites, and estates, for individuals living with mental illness.
answer_text: My Department has policy and legislative responsibility for the Supporting People Programme, which provides housing-related support to those in need, including individuals living with mental illness. The Supporting People Policy Framework (NI) and my answer provided for AQW/40292/22-27 refer to the roles of the Departments, Health and Social Care Trusts and the Housing Executive. The Department of Health is responsible for the physical accommodation.The administrative responsibility for delivering the Supporting People Programme is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Supporting People (SP) programme, administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, provides grant funding for the provision of housing related support and has established arrangements in place to ensure the appropriate use of this funding, including validation and quality monitoring processes. However, the Housing Executive’s responsibilities do not extend to the physical accommodation.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any policy framework operated by his Department in relation to the transfer of individuals assessed as ready for discharge from long-stay inpatient psychiatric units into community-based supported housing; and (ii) whether any cost implications or efficiencies have been identified in association with such transfers.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any current or planned refurbishment or upgrade programmes for supported living accommodation sites for individuals with mental illness, including associated funding and timelines.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The following Supported Housing proposals for clients with mental health issues are currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP):Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned accommodation at 21 Skegoneill Avenue, Belfast to provide 4 self-contained flats. This proposal is included in the 2025/26 SHDP but is expected to slip to 2026/27. Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned property at Dumbarton House (79 Somerton Road), Belfast to provide accommodation for 13 clients plus staff. This proposal is included in the 2026/27 SHDP.For both schemes, associated Business Cases have been endorsed by the Supporting People Strategic Advisory Board, but Choice Housing have not yet submitted Applications for Project Approval (Housing Association Grant funding) to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group (DPG).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his Department’s policy on the quality and suitability of community-based supported housing for individuals living with mental illness; (ii) any current or planned actions to support the availability of such housing within social housing provision; and (iii) how sufficient housing provision is being ensured to facilitate the transfer of individuals from long-stay inpatient psychiatric units into community-based supported housing.
answer_text: My Department has policy and legislative responsibility for the Supporting People Programme, as detailed in the Supporting People Framework, which provides housing related support those in need, including individuals living with mental illness.  The delivery of the Supporting People Programme is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i)There is a wide range of supported housing provision, only some of which is developed through funding from the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP), with housing support funded through the Supporting People (SP) Programme. The SP programme uses a contract management approach to ensure all grant funded schemes deliver high quality housing related support in accordance with funding agreement requirements.  Suitability is assessed by Supporting People Providers through comprehensive needs and risk assessments, ensuring everyone is placed in the most appropriate setting. (ii)The following Supported Housing proposals for clients with mental health issues are currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP):Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned accommodation at 21 Skegoneill Avenue, Belfast to provide 4 self-contained flats. This proposal is included in the 2025/26 SHDP but is expected to slip to 2026/27. Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned property at Dumbarton House (79 Somerton Road), Belfast to provide accommodation for 13 clients plus staff. This proposal is included in the 2026/27 SHDP.Business cases have been formally endorsed for SP funding, but Choice Housing have not yet submitted Applications for Project Approval (Housing Association Grant funding) to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group (DPG). (iii)The assessment of needs for individuals leaving long-stay inpatient psychiatric units is led by Health and Social Care. Any accommodation needs, or needs for housing related support, would be agreed through cross departmental partnership working forums including the Supporting People Strategic Advisory Board.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) progress to date in relation to Action 17 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, including any work currently underway; and (ii) when Action 17 is expected to be prioritised for delivery.
answer_text: (i)Action 17 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 commits to “fully integrate the community and voluntary sector in mental health service delivery with a lifespan approach, including the development of a protocol to make maximum use of the sector’s expertise.” Ernst & Young (EY) LLP was appointed in December 2024 to undertake an independent review of the scope, scale and capacity of the Community & Voluntary (C&V) sector, targeting those organisations that have a ‘constitutional mandate’ to operate in the mental health care arena.  The aim of the review was to identify how best the C&V sector can be fully integrated in the development and delivery of mental health services, ensuring improved outcomes for the population.  The final report was published on 30th January 2026.  (ii) The mental health workforce has been identified as an immediate priority under the Department’s Review of Deliverability of the Mental Health Strategy and this includes the role and contribution of the community and voluntary sector.  A Task and Finish Group, co-chaired by the Department of Health and a senior representative from the community and voluntary sector, is currently being established to consider the recommendations from the EY Review and to agree a prioritised implementation plan for future delivery, taking into account of the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any joint policies and cross-departmental arrangements in place with the Department for Communities in relation to housing provision for individuals transferring from long-stay inpatient psychiatric units into community-based supported housing.
answer_text: Regionally, psychiatric inpatient provision is not identified as long stay inpatient psychiatric units. The Supporting People Policy Framework (NI) 2025, is the updated cross departmental guidance for collaboration between Department of Communities and Department of Health.Trust staff engage on an ongoing basis with Northern Ireland Housing Executive and other accommodation stakeholders on the provision, availability and individual needs of individuals moving from psychiatric units into community based supported accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of individuals clinically assessed as fit for discharge and approved for transition into community-based supported housing who are currently residing in long-stay inpatient psychiatric units, and (ii) the length of time those individuals have been waiting to transition into community-based supported housing, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the proposed Neighbourhood Model of Care will operate in practice, including (a) any governance and accountability arrangements; (b) how consistency and equity of provision across Health and Social Care Trusts will be ensured; (c) the role of the Community and Voluntary Sector in delivery; (d) the anticipated timeline for implementation; and (ii) whether funding to support delivery is still expected to commence from April 2026.
answer_text: In the HSC Reset Plan I made a commitment to developing a neighbourhood model of care, to deliver greater levels of care to our citizens in their local communities.  The proposed Neighbourhood Model of Health and Wellbeing will be built around 17 Integrated Neighbourhood Teams acting as provider alliances. These will operate within the Trust footprint at GP Federation level, serving an average population of 115,000 people. These Teams will bring together professional teams and organisations, representing health, social care and voluntary and community sector services in their respective areas.I intend to publish more detailed policy guidance to support the establishment of the model in early March, alongside the results of the Call for Evidence which shows examples of existing neighbourhood initiatives from various sectors across NI.  Implementation will commence from April 2026, driven by the recently established Neighbourhood Development Programme Board.My Department will continue to engage and work closely with all stakeholders as this work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) the delivery of the priority actions identified by his Department under the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, including (i) the measures implemented; (ii) the outcomes achieved; and (iii) the planned next steps with associated timelines.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 sets out a ten-year vision to improve mental health outcomes in Northern Ireland through 35 actions across three themes: promoting mental wellbeing and resilience, providing the right support at the right time, and new ways of working.Upon its initial publication the NI Mental Health Strategy recognised that implementation of its 35 actions would require a significant investment, estimated at £1.2 billion over a decade based on 2021 costs.•	Unfortunately, to-date, no additional funding has been provided to the Department to support implementation of the Strategy and so existing Departmental resources have been used to progress the 20 actions in the Strategy that are currently underway(ii) The review identified 2 high priority areas (alongside a priority principle) and 4 others classified as medium priority. Additionally, several low or zero cost options are proposed.The review report identifies Action 32 relating to Mental Health workforce and Actions 12 and 27 pertaining to a Regional Crisis Service as immediate priorities for 2026/27 - alongside a broad principle focussed on eradicating inequity within Mental Health Services - should additional funding become available.  These three priorities are proposed as immediate due to their enabling impact on the existing system, and their role as foundational elements for the implementation of medium-priority actions. Significant work has already been undertaken in these areas and further preparatory work can be completed in advance of 2026/27.The review identifies four medium-priority actions, proposed for phased implementation. Two actions: Action 14 (relating to mental health and older people), not yet commenced, and Action 30 (Digital mental health) already commenced are proposed to be prioritised in 2027/28. A further two actions - Action 10 (focused on funding for Child and Adolescent Mental Health) and Action 23 (supporting individuals with severe and enduring mental ill health)—are recommended for prioritisation in 2028/29. Together, these actions represent the next critical steps in progressing beyond immediate priorities.The review identified a number of low/zero cost actions designed to enhance service delivery and user experience. These include:Enhancing the Service User Experience Streamlining Governance, Commissioning, and Planning arrangementsUtilising Outcome Measures to strengthen accountability, inform service development, and support evidence-based decision-making.Improving Transitional Care between Child and Adult Mental Health ServicesEnhancing Carer Involvement (iii) Planning for the development of the 2026/27 Delivery Plan is underway. It is likely that the Plan will focus on the priority areas identified through the Review of Deliverability including what could be achieved if further funding is secured; outline next steps for implementing low or zero cost actions identified through the review; and outline the approach to delivering on the ove

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any progress that has been made since March 2025 in improving endometriosis services and care following the All-Party Group on Women’s Health roundtable, including (i) details of any additional funding committed; (ii) any actions delivered to date; and (iii) when patients can expect improved access to diagnosis and treatment.
answer_text: (i) With regards to additional funding committed so far, a clinical etriage in gynaecology services is being rolled-out using a directtotest model. This assesses patients at the point of referral and directs them straight to diagnostics before a consultant appointment, reducing unnecessary delays. A key development is the expansion of transvaginal scan capacity in all Trusts, enabling women to have investigations completed ahead of their outpatient review.We have invested £1.6 million to implement this model. Subject to further funding, a Phase 2 expansion will increase clinical, nursing and diagnostic capacity, driving further reductions in waiting times.  This is not specifically endometriosis, however as many women are referred to general gynae services, with endometriosis symptoms, it is envisaged that this service will bring improvements to the endometriosis services.(ii)The endometriosis clinical taskandfinish group within the Regional Gynae Collaborative has now completed its work, and a regional clinical pathway has been agreed across Northern Ireland. This pathway covers primary, secondary, regional and tertiary care, specifying the assessments, investigations, diagnostic tests, medications and selfmanagement support women should receive, as well as clear thresholds for referral onwards. Importantly, once treatment is complete, women will be followed up at the most appropriate and local provider.This pathway recognises that endometriosis care must be multidisciplinary. It integrates the role of endometriosis nurses, physiotherapists, psychology, chronic pain services and other relevant surgical specialties, ensuring women receive holistic and coordinated care.To deliver this new model, Trusts are developing business cases to expand local endometriosis services and to establish regional tertiary centres in the Belfast Trust and the Western Trust. However, while the pathway is fully designed, there is insufficient funding at present to implement it in full, thus phase implementation is being planned as funding and staffing allow, prioritising changes that will bring the greatest benefit to patients most quickly.(iii) With regards to improved access to diagnosis and treatment it is not possible to address waits for suspected endometriosis separately from general gynaecology lists, the backlog across gynaecology as a whole is being addressed.Between April and December 2025, £3.9 million was provided in additional nonrecurrent funding for waiting list initiatives, delivered both inhouse and through the independent sector. These initiatives focused on redflag and timecritical assessments and treatments. As a result, 91% of women received their cancer assessment within 9 weeks, and 85% received their inpatient or daycase treatment within 13 weeks. This demonstrates real progress, though we remain determined to improve further.In October 2025, a focused programme was launched to reduce the longest waits—specifically for women waiting more t

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 would provide scope for the planning, funding and delivery of a regional crisis service.
answer_text: Whilst is not possible to fully assess the Department’s position until final Budget settlements have been agreed by the Executive, the Finance Minister’s proposed draft budget means the financial position in future years will be incredibly challenging. The proposed draft budget falls significantly short of my department’s funding requirements and therefore there will not be funding for all that we want or need to do. For 2026/27 at least, we will have to generate funding internally through savings and efficiencies to cover our inescapable commitments, which will include pay awards.  Our financial planning challenge will be exacerbated if any overspend is deducted from our 2026/27 budget. My particular focus is how to deliver the level of savings which would be required for my department, at the same time ensuring safe and quality Health and Social Care Services.However, Mental Health Crisis Services are one of the two immediate Mental Health Strategy priorities for 2026/27, alongside workforce, to ensure people in acute distress receive safe, timely and personcentred support. In the absence of any additional resource, we will continue our efforts to strengthen our crisis response as far as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware of the Health Research Board’s recent €3 million investment in mental health research, including €1 million allocated to an all-island Collaborative Research Network; (ii) to detail any engagement that has taken place in relation to this; and (iii) whether his Department intends to support or facilitate participation in this initiative.
answer_text: I am aware of the €1 million grant allocated to establish an all-island Collaborative Research Network in mental health as part of the recent investment in mental health research in the Republic of Ireland. Officials from my Department have been engaging with the Network leads since May 2025 and the Department was pleased to formally support the application.  The new Research Network will embed lived experience in mental health research through co-production of studies between academics and public involvement, whilst also building capacity and career opportunities for researchers in the area of mental health. The Network will also coordinate mental health research by identifying research gaps and fostering partnerships between academic institutions in Ireland, Northern Ireland and internationally.My Department looks forward to collaborating with partner organisations as part of the network over the coming years to progress areas of mutual research interest

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any current referral pathways into the Regional Trauma Network from (a) Health and Social Care Trusts; (b) General Practice; and (c) the Victims and Survivors Service; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to establishing a single regional referral system.
answer_text: The current referral pathways into the Regional Trauma Network are through:Health and Social Care Trusts’ secondary care services via Encompass;General Practitioners via the Clinical Communication Gateway (CCG) with an indication that the referral is for the attention of the Regional Trauma Network; andThe Victims and Survivors Service (VSS), via a secure system. There are currently no plans for the establishment of a regional referral system.  All referrals are processed via current pathways within Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting times for (a) an initial mental health assessment; (b) psychological therapies; and (c) consultant psychiatric review; (ii) the corresponding waiting times for individuals identified as victims and survivors of the conflict; and (iii) his assessment of the adequacy of interim safeguarding and support arrangements for individuals, including victims and survivors, who are experiencing prolonged waits for assessment or treatment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i)(a)(b)(c)&(ii) The rollout of the Encompass system, a new electronic patient record system, aims to unify health and social care data across Northern Ireland. SPPG and Trusts are unable to detail waiting times currently as data is reported with low confidence. Some improvement is noted in NHSCT & SHSCT, as they are reporting medium confidence.(iii) The Regional Trauma Network (RTN) was established to address the long-term psychological consequences of the “Troubles”/“Conflict”. The HSC element of the RTN is an enhancement of the existing provision of mental health services.  The aim is to improve access to the highest quality trauma services for the population of Northern Ireland by a local trauma team in each HSCT.  The Regional consistent approach to the RTN aims to support victims and survivors on their journey to recovery, connecting specialist trauma care by drawing on existing resources and expertise from the statutory and community and voluntary sectors to enable those experiencing psychological trauma related difficulties to access support, at the right place at the right time. Health and wellbeing Case Managers at Victims & Survivors Service, along with health and wellbeing Case Workers operating within the Community Partner organisations, are an integral component of this RTN model. This model has been designed to support victims and survivors with identification of, and support with their needs.If there are any Adult Safeguarding or Adult Protection issues identified, then Safeguarding policies will be followed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any suicide prevention training programmes that his Department has developed, and rolled out to frontline staff, including (ii) the type, and content of the training; (ii) the method, and duration, of delivery; (iii) the staff groups, and arm’s-length bodies to which it has been provided; and (iv) the number, and geographic spread of staff who have completed it.
answer_text: AnswerIn April 2019, my Department introduced a Suicide and Self-Harm Awareness and Six Point Plan training package for all our frontline staff.  Developed by the Learning and Development Branch and Work Psychology Team in consultation with the Public Health Agency, the programme consists of two parts:Part 1: Suicide and Self-Harm Awareness – covering the Northern Ireland context, risk factors, warning signs, and common misconceptions.Part 2: Introduction to the Six Point Plan – a framework for managing suicide or self-harm disclosures from customers.The course lasts 1.5 to 2 hours and is delivered by Learning and Development trainers, who must hold the City & Guilds Level 4 qualification in Suicide First Aid – Understanding Suicide Intervention.In April 2024, the Six Point Plan was updated in collaboration with Lifeline to include direct referral options.Since April 2019, 5,813 front line staff have completed the training across 35 Jobs and Benefits Offices and centralised benefits branches through a combination of virtual and face to face training deliveryTraining has not been delivered to staff working in Arm’s Length Bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the development and production of an e-learning intervention on suicide prevention, arising from any work undertaken between his Department and the Department of Finance, in the last five years.
answer_text: Development of a suicide prevention training programme in line with the Zero Suicide Alliance training was commenced but never completed due to staffing pressures and prioritisation of the review of Protect Life 2 and development of new action and implementation plans. Further discussions in this regard are planned to develop a new training programme and will seek to identify a resource to take this work forward. The Department for Communities have separately developed and rolled out a suicide prevention training programme to frontline staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department has in place to ensure that (i) publicly-funded mental health initiatives do not direct the public to offices that are not equipped to provide immediate support; and (ii) there are clear referral pathways to appropriate, recognised mental health helplines.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring there is a ‘no wrong door’ approach to members of the public requiring immediate support for their mental health.Implementing the new Regional Crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031. Progress is ongoing in the development of a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service across service areas that are equipped to provide support.The components of the proposed Regional Mental Health Crisis Service are: accessing Mental Health Crisis Services, Primary Care and Interagency Partnership, Community Crisis Service, Mental Health Liaison Service and Crisis Response Home Treatment.A draft lifespan pathway has been presented to all stakeholders including representation of those with lived experience and carers. This draft pathway remains under review and will be developed to ensure there are clear referral pathways to appropriate, recognised mental health helplines and resources including Samaritans, Lifeline and Minding Your Head. Stakeholders are actively engaged in focused work in respect of Right Care Right Person (RCRP) and the roll out of this regionally to ensure that individuals receive the response and care they require from the appropriate aligned service or professional to their need. This will include recognised mental health helplines/resources and services that can provide immediate support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how the proposed entitlement to up to 12 weeks’ neonatal leave and pay, to be provided for in the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill, will align with the provisions of the Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act 2023, including in respect of (a) eligibility; (b) duration; and (c) rate of pay; and (ii) the expected timetable for the commencement of these provisions.
answer_text: I intend to introduce an entitlement to Neonatal Care Leave and Pay on the same basis as that available in Britain, including in respect of eligibility, duration and rate of pay, in my forthcoming Employment Rights Bill. My intention is that this entitlement will become operational in the North of Ireland in 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his Department’s assessment of the Beyond Pills All-Party Parliamentary Group May 2024 report Shifting the Balance Towards Social Interventions: A Call for an Overhaul of the Mental Health System; and (ii) to detail the applicability of the report’s findings for the prescription of antidepressants.
answer_text: (i) The recommendations in the All-Party Parliamentary Group (APPG) report ‘Shifting the Balance Towards Social Interventions’ broadly reflect the vision articulated in the Northern Ireland Mental Health Strategy (MHS) 2021 - 2031 and the Health and Social Care (HSC) Reset Plan. All three share a vision for a person-centred, recovery-focused system that prioritises early intervention, prevention, and community-based support. The HSC Reset Plan reinforces this through its commitment to a neighbourhood model of care, integrated partnerships with the Voluntary and Community sector, and addressing wider social determinants of health. The Mental Health Strategy recognises the value of non-pharmaceutical supports such as talking therapies, social prescribing and the work of the Recovery Colleges, all of which are established to varying degrees across Northern Ireland. These approaches reflect the APPG’s call to reduce reliance on clinical interventions alone and deliver holistic, equitable mental health services through collaborative, community-driven models.  However, it is important to emphasise that medication remains a vital and essential component of treatment for many individuals - particularly those with long-term conditions - and this is clearly detailed within the Mental Health Strategy as a key element in supporting recovery when used appropriately.While the All-Party Parliamentary Group report calls for significant investment to deliver its ambitions, delivery of the MHS faces substantial funding constraints, as highlighted in the recent Review of the Deliverability of the Strategy’s Actions (2026 - 29). Addressing the social determinants of mental health remains critical, and I will continue to work with Ministerial colleagues through the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention to take that forward.(ii) The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) is the UK competent authority for medicines and is responsible for the licensing and monitoring of all medicines on the UK market, ensuring they meet their high standards on safety, efficacy and quality. The MHRA rigorously monitors the safety of antidepressants from a wide range of sources – including from clinical trials, international databases, and the Yellow Card scheme. Should new evidence warrant changes to product information or additional safety advice, the MHRA will take appropriate regulatory action. In addition to this, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) considers the best available evidence when producing guidance for the NHS and wider health and social care system. My Department has a formal link with the NICE whereby NICE guidance is locally reviewed for applicability and, where appropriate, endorsed for implementation under Health and Social Care (HSC) arrangements. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland (NI) has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the val

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current funding arrangements in each Health and Social Care Trust for (a) family practitioners working with parents of premature babies; and (b) associated home-based post-discharge programmes, indicating in each case whether this funding is recurrent or non-recurrent; (ii) any steps his Department is taking to ensure sustainable recurrent funding for these services across all Health and Social Care Trusts; and (iii) any assessment his Department has made of the impact on families, and on maternity and neonatal services, of the absence of such fu
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact on parents with children born prematurely.  Each of the Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to an external voluntary and community organisation Tiny Life, who offer specialised resources and support.Information / Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.TinyLife’s services are delivered across all five HSC Trusts and include a Breast Pump Loan service, Neonatal Unit visits, Home support, TinyTime Groups, Baby Massage Classes, TinyGym (NHSCT only), TinyLearners: Training and support to early years education professionals and TinyStart: A home-based parenting education programme from birth to pre-school. In addition, parents with children born prematurely are provided with social work  and financial support. Each of the Health and Social Care Trusts may offer the following:Open access for parents with visiting for families. Free care parking and meal vouchers and rooming in/bedroom facilities. An area which allows play and refreshments to be made. Each cot space have recliners ensuring parents are comfortable.Parents are offered the support of the bereavement midwife team for emotional and financial support through the bereavement fund.Chaplain services are available 24/7.  Social workers to do family needs assessment in relation to travel expenses, housing and provision of essentials for discharge as required.The Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF) framework, is our universal Child Health Promotion Programme in Northern Ireland. This framework is dedicated to ensuring that every child and young person receives the best possible start in life.  The universal Healthy Child, Healthy Future programme is provided to the total population of babies, children and young people aged 0-19 years, irrespective of need. The HCHF programme offers set contacts to each family with the aim to identify health needs. As part of this programme a holistic assessment which includes screening and surveillance is undertaken. When need is identified, it provides early intervention to ameliorate the potential early negative impact of any physical, social, or emotional factors. Some children and families will receive a targeted service, e.g. babies born pre-term (as per NICE NG 72). It will also ensure that the needs of parents of premature or low birth weight babies will be identified, and appropriate support offered.Health Visiting which is recurrently funded  offers this universal service in each Trust and provides crucial support to both the baby and the parents in the postnatal period including growth and developmental assessments, feeding advice, address specific health concerns related to prematurity, and provide mental and emotional wellbeing support. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 32896/22-27, to detail (i) any assessment as to whether full and safe removal surgery for the types of implants used in the north of Ireland constitutes a surgical super specialism and whether such surgery can be safely provided within local services; (ii) any assessment of the effectiveness of services for patients who have experienced complications from mesh procedures since the 2018 pause; (iii) any assessment of the operation of the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) scheme, including referrals of patients to Dr Sohier Elneil; (iv) the rationale
answer_text: Mesh removal treatment is provided on a regional basis by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) and assurance can be provided that the Regional Mesh Service is being provided safely.  However, following review at the BHSCT’s Regional Mesh Service, should a patient present with complex treatment needs then expertise is sought outside of Northern Ireland via the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process.  The ECR process occurs when my Department approves an HSC consultant’s request to transfer a patient to a provider outside NI for assessment or treatment which the consultant considers necessary, but which is not available through NI HSC facilities.  This may be either because the treatment needed is of a specialist nature which is not available in NI, or because there is a clinical reason why the local service is not appropriate.  The provider is chosen by the local consultant using their clinical judgement.  The ECR process remains ongoing and in specific relation to mesh removal treatment, since the development of the Regional Mesh Service a very small number of patients have been referred under ECR arrangements to UK NHS providers, as determined by their local treating consultant and their specific treatment needs.  Patients referred outside of NI for special mesh surgery under ECR arrangements, can expect to have their treatment and travel arrangements, (in accordance with patient travel policy guidance), supported by my Department.The budget for 2025/26 remains under extreme pressure and at present there are no plans to initiate a specific scheme which will enable patients to be reimbursed for any mesh surgery undertaken outside of NI.  In order to make best use of available resources it is expected that patients will receive treatment within local HSC services in the first instance, with onward referral outside of NI via the ECR process when appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current arrangements for the collection and reporting of data on premature births and neonatal outcomes in each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT); (ii) how any discrepancies identified between HSCTs in how premature births are coded and reported; (iii) how the encompass programme will be used to standardise and improve data collection for premature and vulnerable babies across all HSCTs; and (iv) the timescales for delivering a single regional dataset to support planning, service improvement and research.
answer_text: (i) the current arrangements for the collection and reporting of data on premature births and neonatal outcomes in each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT)The BadgerNet system is the primary mode of data collection for infants admitted to a neonatal unit and is in use in all five HSCTs.  Data is collected on all infants admitted to a neonatal unit, not just infants who have been born prematurely. The BadgerNet database is mirrored by the Data Warehouse which is hosted by Business Services Organisation (BSO) and the Neonatal Network Northern Ireland (NNNI) provides summary clinical activity and key neonatal outcome reports for the region, using data which is held within the Data Warehouse.EPIC is the new electronic record that replaces paper-based notes within each of the HSCTs and is now fully rolled out across Northern Ireland.  There is limited transfer of data from EPIC to the BadgerNet system. At present, BadgerNet requires manual updating in order to provide the daily regional activity and staffing reports to Commissioners and summary NNNI annual reports, as EPIC does not provide this information.It is anticipated that from Spring 2026, all neonatal units in Northern Ireland will be submitting data to the National Neonatal Audit Programme (NNAP), which is hosted by the Royal College of Paediatrics & Child Health (RCPCH) on behalf of Healthcare Quality Improvement Partnership (HQIP). The NNAP permits national benchmarking of 10 key neonatal care processes and infant outcomes. At present, only data contained in the BadgerNet database can be transferred to NNAP, as there are no direct links from EPIC to NNAP to facilitate the information flow.  (ii) how any discrepancies identified between HSCTs in how premature births are coded and reported	There are pre-set diagnostic codes used within both the BadgerNet system and the EPIC system.  There are no current mechanisms in place to explore any discrepancies.(iii) how the encompass programme will be used to standardise and improve data collection for premature and vulnerable babies across all HSCTs	EPIC has now been rolled out across all HSCTs within Northern Ireland and has digitised health and care records, by bringing together information from various existing systems that do not currently communicate effectively. This has reduced: the time needed to access information;the need for hard copy paper records; andduplication of orders. At present, the current status of the integration between EPIC and BadgerNet is that only a limited number of the required admission details for each infant can be transferred. This occurs in a semi-automatic manner. Efforts to improve to the volume of data that can be transferred from EPIC to BadgerNet are ongoing within the Encompass Clinical Pathway group for Paediatrics. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current arrangements for neonatal transfers and other specialist care where babies are treated in hospitals in the south of Ireland; (ii) the protocols in place for sharing medical records and clinical information in these cases; (iii) how information on parental supports and funding entitlements is communicated to the families concerned; and (iv) any steps his Department is taking, including through the North-South Ministerial Council health structures, to ensure that medical records and parental entitlements follow the baby when care is provide
answer_text: (i) The transfer of neonates from Northern Ireland to and from hospitals in the Republic of Ireland for care and treatment are facilitated by the Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval Service (NISTAR).  NISTAR is a dedicated service that facilitates the transfer of adults, children and neonates.  The service is primarily for the transfer of patients between intensive care level facilities in the north.  It is also formally commissioned to undertake transfers mainly of babies and children where required to hospitals in the south of Ireland.(ii)  A service level agreement is currently being developed which will ensure a formal structure is in place for the safe, seamless transfer of obstetric and neonatal cases from Northern Ireland (NI) to the Republic of Ireland (RoI) for those times when there is insufficient capacity within the Neonatal Network in Northern Ireland.  When there are such capacity issues, maternity hospitals in Northern Ireland may seek the transfer of the mother and/or neonate to RoI.  This agreement will be accompanied by a standard operating procedure that each NI hospital should adhere to before transfer.  The RoI maternity hospitals will provide appropriate maternity and neonatal care to the NI pregnant woman or neonates subject to the same professional standards and clinical governance arrangements as they would in respect of a RoI resident.  Referral documentation should be completed by the NI referral site and transferred with the patient in order to ensure safe, appropriate high-quality care is delivered in the RoI site.  Discharge documentation should be completed and any follow-up requirements should be outlined in a written communication to facilitate the continuum of care between RoI and NI.  This documentation should be transferred with the patient if returning to hospital of intended birth or sent via mail to referring hospital if patient is being discharged home.  (iii) Neonatal patients who are referred to hospitals in the Republic of Ireland receive the same entitlements as any other patient referred outside of Northern Ireland for treatment.  Detailed travel guidance is made available to the patient or family in the case of a neonatal baby.At point of referral, the respective Trust should advise and share information with the family concerned and there is also information on how to contact the Patient Travel Team for advice and guidance via a website link although all paediatric travel is manged by the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children’s Patient Experience Team.  A patient information leaflet guidance document is made available and can also be accessed via the HSC website: Patients travelling outside Northern Ireland for treatment - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) (iv) As outlined above, a service level agreement is currently being developed which will ensure a formal structure is in place for the safe, seamless transfer of obstetric and neonatal cases from Northern

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment made of the clinical safety of the current alternatives to admission to a dedicated Mother and Baby Unit for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis; (ii) whether it is considered clinically safe to treat a woman with postpartum psychosis at home in the absence of access to a Mother and Baby Unit; and (iii) any assessment made on the short and long-term impact on both mother and baby of separating them when the mother is admitted to a general psychiatric ward.
answer_text: The clinical advice I have received to date indicates that the current proposed model—delivered through integrated community and acute services—is the safest and most appropriate option available at present however as you are aware, the ability to take forward the development of the Mother and Baby Unit will remain subject to the necessary funding being identified.(i) Severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis, is recognised as requiring specialist, high-intensity intervention. Clinical guidance indicates that dedicated Mother and Baby Units (MBUs) provide the optimal and safest inpatient environment, as they enable co-admission of mother and infant alongside specialist perinatal psychiatric care. In the absence of an MBU in Northern Ireland, women who require inpatient treatment are admitted to general adult psychiatric wards. Clinical assessments undertaken by healthcare professionals acknowledge that, while these wards can provide acute psychiatric management, they do not offer the specialised facilities or expertise available in MBUs. However, general psychiatric wards have received additional funding to create a more family-friendly environment and to provide baby-feeding equipment, with the aim of improving support for mothers who require admission. These enhancements mitigate some limitations but are regarded as interim measures rather than clinically equivalent alternatives to an MBU. (ii) Postpartum psychosis is treated as a psychiatric emergency. Clinical guidance does not consider home-based treatment to be a safe alternative for women with acute postpartum psychosis. Home management is only considered in exceptional circumstances where risk is assessed as low and where intensive, specialist perinatal support and support by home treatment teams is available. In general, the condition warrants inpatient care. In the absence of an MBU, admission to a general psychiatric ward is used where clinically necessary.(iii) Clinical evidence indicates that separation of a mother and infant in the early postnatal period can have both short- and long-term effects. Short-term impacts include disruption to bonding and attachment, difficulties with breastfeeding, and heightened distress for both mother and baby. Longer-term potential impacts include challenges in the development of secure attachment, adverse developmental or emotional outcomes for the child, and the risk of prolonged maternal mental-health difficulties. MBUs are specifically designed to mitigate these risks by enabling mothers to remain with their infants while receiving specialist psychiatric care.  I remain committed to evidence-based decision-making and any option taken forward must provide a safe, appropriate environment for those women requiring inpatient care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how the Royal College of Psychiatrists’ perinatal mental health guidelines have informed his assessment of current provision for mothers and babies; (ii) whether he has sought specific advice on the clinical safety of establishing an interim inpatient Mother and Baby facility pending a permanent Unit; and (iii) whether any concerns about interim provision relate to the risk of an interim arrangement becoming permanent, rather than to the clinical safety of a dedicated interim ward.
answer_text: I am fully committed to delivering safe, accessible, and high-quality maternal mental health care until such time as a permanent Mother and Baby Unit can be established. Consideration of the Royal College of Psychiatrists’ perinatal mental health guidelines are key in the assessment of current provision for mothers and babies and, specifically, in relation to the scoping work undertaken by my officials looking at the feasibility of an interim specialist unit pending the progression of a permanent Mother and Baby Unit.  Clinical advice on the safety of any proposal to make interim or permanent provision for an MBU is of paramount importance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) an updated timeline for completion and approval of the Outline Business Case for a Mother and Baby Unit; (ii) the current estimated capital and revenue costs for the Unit, including how these compare to the previously cited figure of £11 million; and (iii) whether the Unit continues to be treated as a top priority within his Department’s capital programme.
answer_text: (i)  The Belfast Trust have approved the Outline Business Case (OBC) for the Mother and Baby Unit.  The OBC has been submitted to the Department for consideration.  Departmental approval will be subject to a commissioner’s letter of support being received confirming that the revenue costs for the unit are affordable.  (ii)  The anticipated capital cost of the proposed Mother and Baby Unit is £7m.  The current figure is less than the previously cited figure of £11m as a result of the schedule of accommodation area being reduced by 400m2 following an internal review undertaken by Departmental officials.   The anticipated revenue costs excluding inflation are £2.45m per annum.(iii)  My ability to commit funding for this project will depend on the Department’s capital allocation from the upcoming budget process and the identification of sufficient recurrent funding to meet any additional resource costs associated with the facility.  I can assure you that this project is high up my list of priorities and I am keen to progress it as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any ongoing discussions taking place with his counterpart in Dublin on an all-island agreement to facilitate access for women and their babies to Mother and Baby Unit care, including in the event that such a Unit is established in Dublin in advance of one in Belfast; and (ii) whether there is any group or mechanism in place to collect and analyse data on perinatal mental health services.
answer_text: My officials have continued to liaise with RoI counterparts to discuss their plans to establish MBUs with a view to exploring co-operation. The priority for my Department is getting mothers access to the right facility as soon as possible.There are established mechanisms within Northern Ireland for the collection and analysis of data relating specifically to perinatal mental-health services. Each Health and Social Care Trust’s Community Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Team systematically records information on service access, clinical assessments and treatment outcomes. Furthermore, a regional data-reporting system is currently under development. Upon completion, this system will provide a standardised framework for data collection and will enable the publication of consistent metrics on service performance and outcomes across perinatal mental-health services in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department is taking to support Health and Social Care Trusts in reducing waiting times for outpatient and surgical gynaecology services.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners.  This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels which are clinically led.  It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this.  If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service had delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations at a cost in-year of £1.07m.  When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Trusts have established a Gynaecology Provider Collaborative - February 2025.  As part of this process, two Collaborative sub-groups have been established, namely:  A Clinical Standardisation Group – with Clinical and Director/Assista

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to ensure that primary care practitioners (i) make clinically appropriate referrals to gynaecology services; and (ii) ensure timely access to diagnostic investigations for patients presenting with symptoms indicative of menstrual health conditions.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including gynaecology and menstrual health conditions.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.  Action plans are being developed to address waiting times and implement relevant Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, my Department secured and additional £3.9m of non-recurrent funding to provide waiting list initiatives, both internally and in partnership with the independent sector.  The aim of these initiatives is to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients appointments are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries which currently  cannot be provided in Primary Care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the passage of the Mother and Baby Unit motion in the Assembly on 7 October 2025, to provide an update on the progress of the scoping exercise being undertaken by his officials to identify an appropriate interim space for a Mother and Baby Unit.
answer_text: Officials have been working at pace scoping options for potential interim Mother and Baby Unit (MBU) provision while continuing to progress plans for a permanent facility. This work includes a detailed assessment of feasibility, refurbishment requirements, capital and revenue implications, and most importantly, clinical considerations. I expect briefing on the outcome of this work imminently. This work is being prioritised to ensure that mothers requiring specialist mental health inpatient care can access safe and appropriate services as quickly as possible, whilst keeping their baby with them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the schools that have been selected as part of the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme pilot scheme for covered sports area.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the timeline for delivering a final draft of the Anti-Poverty Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the new Breastfeeding Strategy will be published; and (ii) what (a) measurable targets; (b) resource allocations; and (c) oversight mechanisms, will be included to ensure that breastfeeding continuation rates improve equitably, given the persistently low rates in more deprived areas.
answer_text: The Breastfeeding Strategy ‘Breastfeeding – A Great Start for Northern Ireland (2013-2023)’ ended in June 2024 after a one-year extension. My Department completed a review of the Strategy and found that while there has been considerable progress made when it comes to professional development, service standards, data quality, and support for mothers and babies, there are also priority areas that still need to be addressed.In July 2024, I made the decision to build on the strategic direction already set out in the previous strategy and develop a Breastfeeding Action Plan. This Action Plan is currently being co-developed by my Department and the Public Health Agency, with input from a wide range of key stakeholders. This collaborative approach will help determine the actions that will most benefit women, babies and families in Northern Ireland.Unfortunately, due to ongoing budgetary pressures there will be no additional resources associated with this Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the premia payment made to St Mary’s University College.
answer_text: St Mary’s and Stranmillis University Colleges make a huge contribution to our society. During the current academic year, my Department will provide funding for over 1,160 student teachers at the institutions which also deliver other courses, high quality research and educational resources. The University Colleges play a very positive role in their local communities. For example, St Mary’s hosts events throughout the year in partnership with Féile; and Stranmillis engages through the delivery of public events, including the annual Éamon Phoenix memorial lecture.Like all public services, both institutions are under significant financial pressure. Their small size and specialist nature makes it particularly challenging for them to make efficiencies. That is why St Mary’s and Stranmillis receive premia in recognition of their status; an approach which is common across these islands.In order to support the long-term sustainability of St Mary’s and Stranmillis, I have decided to allocate each institution an additional £400,000 during the current academic year. This equates to an additional 33% of the premia already paid by my Department in recognition of their small and specialist nature.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail key Executive delivery points since February 2024.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps being taken to expand the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme to recognise the needs of mesh injured women; and (ii) whether the scheme applies to treatment in England, including highly specialised mesh removal surgery.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to be reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  The Scheme, launched 2 June 2025, applies the following strict eligibility criteria:•	Patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland;•	Patients who are on a treatment or surgical waiting list within HSC services in Northern Ireland for 52 weeks or more.Unfortunately, Mesh removal surgery is a very specialist service not currently available in NI and therefore unavailable through the waiting list scheme. That said, NI patients do have access to Mesh removal treatment if clinically appropriate, on an equitable basis to that of patients in GB, through the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process. This is managed through SPPG Hospital Care Directorate.The Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process enables local Trust consultants to seek funding approval to transfer a named patient to a provider outside Northern Ireland.  This applies in cases where there is requirement for assessment or treatment which the consultant considers necessary, but is not available in local health and social care facilities.  This may be either because the treatment needed is of a specialist nature, or because there is a clinical reason why the local service is not appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been carried out into the long-term health risks associated with microplastic exposure from transvaginal mesh implants.
answer_text: Evidence on the potential long-term health effects of microplastic exposure from transvaginal mesh implants remains limited. Currently, the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) has not identified any new safety concerns in relation to microplastic exposure with these products.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether non-disclosure agreements have been used in mesh related settlements; and (ii) to outline any measures that are in place to ensure transparency for patients and survivors.
answer_text: I can confirm that non-disclosure agreements have not been used by Health & Social Care organisations in relation to mesh-related settlements.		My Department has provided supporting guidance to healthcare professionals to raise awareness of information resources and support services available to patients in Northern Ireland who are exploring options for the treatment of hernia or who are experiencing adverse side-effects or complications following  surgery, recording their concerns and preferences along the treatment pathway, to reach a shared understanding of expectations and limitations of the available treatment options.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what improvements his Department intends to make to gynaecological services across the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There is currently significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.To support this, the Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, which includes representation from Trusts, the Public Health Agency, and General Practitioners. This forum is overseeing ongoing service improvements and the development of gynaecology service pathways across primary, intermediate, secondary, and tertiary care levels. It is also responsible for implementing the recommendations of the “Getting It Right First Time” (GIRFT) Gynaecology Report.Most referral pathways begin in primary care, and the gynaecology pathways being developed reflect this. If a GP is unable to assess or treat a patient locally, the patient may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service—if they meet the criteria—or to secondary care gynaecology services.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional. In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service. Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology. This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.As of 30 September 2025, the Enhanced Primary Care Service has delivered 1,567 face-to-face appointments and 587 remote consultations. When fully operational, it is expected to manage approximately 6,072 patient contacts annually (including both face-to-face and remote appointments).Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust. Additional non-recurrent funding has been approved for waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector—which will enable over 10,000 additional patients to be assessed, diagnosed, and treated in 2025/26, at an in-year cost of more than £4.2 million. This will help reduce waiting times for patients requiring red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments. However, I acknowledge that the current financial constraints may limit progress for non-red flag or non-urgent conditions such as endometriosis, unless clinically indicated.The Elective Care Framework has also provided additional funding to address service gaps in gynaecology. My officials will continue to explore options to strengthen pathways and improve care for women with gynaecological conditions. Some recommendations will require investment, while others can be achieved through modernisation and reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department’s plans for a redress scheme for women harmed by transvaginal mesh, including (i) whether provision will be made for reimbursement of surgery carried out in specialist centres outside the North of Ireland, such as in the United States; and (ii) whether consideration will be given to mirroring the approach taken in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by Pelvic Mesh implants in the “Hughes Report”, which was published on 07 February 2024.  Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  As such, the matter of financial redress for those affected by pelvic mesh implants remains a reserved matter.  The three devolved administrations, including Northern Ireland, are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC).  The UK Government has not as yet taken a decision on this redress.  However, my Department continues to engage with counterparts in England, Scotland, and Wales to ensure that the recommendations of the Hughes Report are considered.I wrote to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health and Mental Health at the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) in September 2024, requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and requesting assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations would take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions. Baroness Merron, replied on 13 November 2024, stating that the Government needed time to carefully consider the Hughes Report before coming to a decision.  The Minister acknowledged they are keen to work closely with officials across the UK in considering the Report’s recommendations.In the meantime, you will be aware that patients requiring MESH removal surgery are managed in the Belfast Trust.  As such we do not routinely require women to transfer to other providers for this surgery. However, if a patient requires a type of surgery which is not available in the Belfast Trust, perhaps due the level of complexity, the patient’s consultant will be aware that patients can be referred for treatment outside Northern Ireland via the Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) route and processes are in place to allow this to occur.
