question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail what proportion of an individual’s PAYE and National Insurance contributions is allocated to NHS dental treatment; and (ii) whether there is any mechanism for individuals to reclaim this portion if they are unable to access NHS dental services.
answer_text: (i) 	No direct mechanism currently exists for allocating an individual's taxes to a specific part of the Health Service. The Assembly controls spending but not the collection or earmarking of individual taxes.  Taxes are collected by HMRC on behalf of the UK Government. The Block Grant is made to NI by Treasury as a separate process. There is no direct link between what an individual pays and how much goes to health services in Northern Ireland.(ii)	In Northern Ireland, individuals cannot reclaim National Insurance contributions allocated to NHS dental treatment. NI contributions are pooled into a general fund that supports a wide range of public services, including the NHS, and are not itemised or refundable for specific services like dental care.However, if someone paid for NHS dental treatment and later discovered they were eligible for free or reduced-cost care, they may be able to claim a refund of the dental charges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on late-night public transport.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plan to maximise the economic potential of dual market access, as recommended by the Independent Review of the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: The recommendation that both the UK Government and the Executive continue to emphasise and publicise the commercial benefits of dual market access is one of several made in the Murphy Report.  The report is currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the role his Department is playing, in partnership with the Department of Justice, in engaging with organisations such as DAWG Suicide Prevention to support the mental health and wellbeing of prisoners and former prisoners.
answer_text: My Department is committed to improving the mental health and wellbeing for both individuals receiving healthcare services in prison and those individuals transitioning between healthcare services in prison and those in the community setting.  The Strategic Planning and Performance Group in conjunction with the Public Health Agency are responsible for the planning and commissioning of healthcare services in prison.  A multidisciplinary planning and commissioning group for healthcare within the criminal justice system is well established and is co-chaired by both organisations.    The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust is commissioned to provide a range of healthcare services, including mental health services, across all prisons in Northern Ireland. Together with the Northern Ireland Prison Service, the Trust seeks to support those particularly at risk of suicide and self-harm though a joint Suicide and Self-Harm Strategy which is operationalised through the Supporting People at Risk Evolution (SPAR Evo) approach. The strategy promotes awareness of positive mental health and the reduction of stigma associated with self-harm and suicidal thoughts and behaviours. To ensure continuity of care is maximised the Healthcare in Prison team work collaboratively with the Community Mental Health and Addictions Services for those open to the service on transition.  I can confirm that Healthcare in Prison is part of the regional Towards Zero Suicide project, which is led by a co-ordinator based within Northern Ireland Prisons. This project promotes a whole prison approach to supporting those at risk of suicide and self-harm and does so in collaboration with a wide range of stakeholders including community and voluntary sector organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme public consultation.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he is aware that parents in receipt of Universal Credit have been refused entitlements to Uniform Grants and Free School Meals for their children.
answer_text: For households receiving Universal Credit, there has always been a net household income threshold applied to determine eligibility for free school meals and uniform grants in Northern Ireland. On 8 July, I announced that from the 2025/2026 academic year, this income threshold for families in receipt of Universal Credit who apply for free school meals and uniform grants, will rise from £15,000 to £15,390. There will be some households in receipt of Universal Credit whose income exceeds the threshold and therefore will not be entitled to free school meals and uniform grants. Any household in receipt of Universal Credit, with an income below the threshold, that has not been able to successfully apply for free school meals or uniform grants, should seek the assistance of the Education Authority. Guidance and contact details are available at https://www.eani.org.uk/parents/pupil-applications-and-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grants. For families with an income above the income threshold, I would encourage them to contact the “Make the Call Service” to check if they are receiving all the benefits, services and supports that they are entitled to. Details of this service can be found at www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what enhanced public awareness campaigns are currently being delivered or planned to promote (a) HPV vaccination; and (b) cervical screening; (ii) how these campaigns are being tailored to target high-risk or under-screened age groups, such as women aged 30–34; and (iii) how the impact of these campaigns is being monitored and reported.
answer_text: In the absence of a dedicated budget for mass media campaigns, the PHA has adopted a range of targeted, community-based, and digital strategies to promote cervical screening and HPV vaccination. In response to declining uptake rates across all childhood vaccination programmes, the PHA established the Improving Childhood Vaccination Uptake Working Group (ICVUWG). As part of this group, various initiatives have taken place to promote public awareness of HPV vaccination. This includes working with charities such as Cancer Focus; the development of online awareness sessions for other professional bodies (such as teaching staff, SureStart and community pharmacies); and carrying out visits to secondary schools to speak to young people about the importance of HPV vaccination. The PHA also engages with further education organisations to promote vaccinations prior to young people starting further education.The PHA has developed resources for professionals, young people and parents. This includes the development of a video promoting teenage vaccinations (including HPV), which is also available in other languages. Further developments to this are planned.The PHA also carries out the following activities to promote vaccinations across Northern Ireland:Production of printed and online patient information to support informed consent (e.g. leaflets and posters). These are distributed to those involved in vaccinating and are available on the PHA website.Production of professional information and training, including professional factsheets, training slides and webinars with vaccinating staff, which includes how to promote uptake.Promotion of the programmes through unpaid methods using PHA’s own social media page and the issue of press releases. The HPV vaccine will again be offered at the Belfast Pride 2025 event, primarily focusing on the ‘at risk’ programme for gay, bisexual and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM).To promote public awareness of cervical screening, the PHA has updated all public facing information relating to the change in the screening process (information leaflets, websites) and has delivered training sessions to primary care staff so that they are equipped to inform women and answer any questions. There is ongoing media engagement and posts on social media to promote the programme and the change to primary HPV (pHPV) testing. A PHA social medial campaign to promote launch of pHPV testing ran from 11 Dec 2023 to 8 Jan 2024.  This included explanation around pHPV changes, list of Questions & Answers, and several posts encouraging uptake of the programme, reinforcing the cancer screening saves lives message.During 2024/25, the PHA collaborated with Cancer Research UK (CRUK) in the design of a public information campaign to promote participation in the three cancer screening programmes. This wide reaching 6-week multi-media campaign launched in February 2025 and included TV, radio, social media and outdoor advertising across Northern Irel

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what targeted outreach programmes are in place to address low (a) cervical screening; and (b) vaccination rates, in areas of social deprivation, including the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) how these programmes are working to reduce barriers and health inequalities; and (iii) whether additional funding or support will be made available to expand such initiatives.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) what targeted outreach programmes are in place to address low (a) cervical screening; and (b) vaccination rates, in areas of social deprivation, including West Belfast; (ii) how these programmes are working to reduce barriers and health inequalities; and (iii) whether additional funding or support will be made available to expand such initiatives.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to addressing health inequalities through targeted outreach and community engagement, to address low cervical screening and vaccination rates. In relation to Cervical screening, the PHA continues to commission the Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA) to promote informed choice across the three cancer screening programmes via a peer led community-based programme. The programme aims to provide information and improve knowledge of the benefits of screening and specifically targets groups where rates of participation in screening is likely to be lower, including those impacted by health inequalities and living in deprived areas. In 2024/25, 118 sessions were delivered to community groups across Northern Ireland (29 of these specifically on cervical screening, of which 11 were in the Belfast Trust area) with a total 1,298 attendees.  An additional 125 sessions (28 of these on cervical screening of which 7 were in the Belfast Trust area) were delivered to groups with additional needs, with a total 1,364 attendees.The WRDA have significant reach with marginalised groups and a strategy of involving service users in the planning and delivery of all sessions, ensuring they are tailored for each group.  During 2024/25, WRDA attended 37 health events across Northern Ireland to promote informed choice in cancer screening. This included partnering with the PHA Health Improvement team, and several community partners, working on the new “Live Better” initiative targeting areas of health inequalities, attending health events in Moor in Derry/Londonderry and Lower Shankill and Lower Falls in Belfast.  In addition, the Community Pharmacy ‘Living Well’ campaign, focused on cancer screening in April and May 2024. The objectives of the campaign were: To raise awareness of cancer screening programmes to all who are eligible.To raise awareness of the benefits of attending cancer screening when invited.To contribute to creating confidence in cancer screening programmes and encourage uptake.The PHA has also initiated a broader programme of work aimed at addressing health inequalities across all population screening programmes in Northern Ireland. This includes early-stage exploratory research into approaches adopted elsewhere in the UK and the Republic of Ireland. An initial internal stakeholder event has been held to bring together key areas of work and explore opportunities for a more integrated approach. The focus spans the four key domains of health inequality: inclusion health and vulnerable groups, socio-economically deprived populations, individuals with protected characteristics, and geographic disparities. Plans are also underway to conduct wider stakeholder engagement in the coming months to help shape the future direction of this work.With regards to vaccination programmes, the PHA carries out various activities to promote vaccinations across Northern Ireland, including:Production of printed and online patient informati

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current HPV vaccination coverage rates in West Belfast, (a) by gender; and (b) age group; (ii) what local plans are in place in West Belfast to achieve the World Health Organisation target of 90 per cent HPV vaccination coverage; and (iii) how his Department is engaging with local schools, health services and community groups to support these efforts.
answer_text: Data detailing HPV vaccination coverage rates is not available for West Belfast.  The PHA publishes the annual HPV vaccine coverage, by Health and Social Care Trust, of gender and school year group.  The latest data available is for the 2023/24 academic year and can be viewed at: Annual HPV vaccine 2023-2024|Vaccination coverage|PHA website.  An alternative breakdown of uptake information could not be provided but work is being undertaken to allow the PHA to produce various breakdown of data in the future. HPV vaccination is offered by school nursing teams to all pupils while in school year 9.  A further offer is made to any pupils who remain unvaccinated in school year 10.  Individuals remain eligible to receive the HPV vaccine up until they are 25 years of age and they can request the HPV vaccine from their GP, if they missed the vaccine for any reason while in school years 9 and 10.  The PHA carries out the following activities to promote vaccinations in general across Northern Ireland:Production of printed and online patient information to support informed consent (e.g. leaflets and posters). These are distributed to those involved in vaccinating and are available on the PHA website.Production of professional information and training, including professional factsheets, training slides and webinars with vaccinating staff, which includes how to promote uptake.Promotion of the programmes through unpaid methods using PHA’s own social media page and the issue of press releasesIn response to recently declining uptake rates across all childhood vaccination programmes, the PHA established the Improving Childhood Vaccination Uptake Working Group (ICVUWG). As part of this group, a number of activities to improve uptake have been undertaken, such as working with charities (e.g. Cancer Focus to promote awareness of HPV vaccination), the development of online awareness sessions for other professional groups/bodies (such as teaching staff, SureStart and community pharmacies), and carrying out visits to secondary schools to speak to young people about the importance of vaccination, including HPV. The PHA routinely engages with the school nursing service through the Childhood Immunisation and Vaccinations Group (CVIG) to support them in the delivery of the service. As part of this group, vaccination uptake is routinely monitored, and service updates are provided. The PHA also engages with further education organisations to promote vaccinations prior to young people starting further education.The PHA has developed resources for professionals, young people and parents. This includes the development of a video promoting teenage vaccinations (including HPV), which is also available in other languages. Further developments to this are planned.The HPV vaccine will again be offered at the Belfast Pride 2025 event, primarily focusing on the ‘at risk’ programme for gay, bisexual and other men who have sex with men (GBMSM).

The PHA carries out the following activities to promote vaccinations in general across Northern Ireland:Production of printed and online patient information to support informed consent (e.g. leaflets and posters). These are distributed to those involved in vaccinating and are available on the PHA website.Production of professional information and training, including professional factsheets, training slides and webinars with vaccinating staff, which includes how to promote uptake.Promotion of the programmes through unpaid methods using PHA’s own social media page and the issue of press releasesIn response to recently declining uptake rates across all childhood vaccination programmes, the PHA established the Improving Childhood Vaccination Uptake Working Group (ICVUWG). As part of this group, a number of activities to improve uptake have been undertaken, such as working with charities (e.g. Cancer Focus to promote awareness of HPV vaccination), the development of online awareness sessions for other professional groups/bodies (such as teaching staff, SureStart and community pharmacies), and carrying out visits to secondary schools to speak to young people about the importance of vaccination, including HPV. The PHA routinely engages with the school nursing service through the Childhood Immunisation and Vaccinations Group (CVIG) to support them in the delivery of the service. As part of this group, vaccination uptake is routinely monitored, and service updates are provided. The PHA also engages with further education organisations to promote vaccinations prior to young people starting further education.The PHA has developed resources for professionals, young people and parents. This includes the development of a video promoting teenage vaccinations (including HPV), which is also available in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to ensure the continued accuracy and completeness of Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service waiting time data during the implementation of Encompass, across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department is aware that the implementation of the Encompass system, along with changes to data recording practices, has disrupted CAMHS data reporting. In response, my officials are working closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure the accuracy of recorded information and to restore robust and reliable reporting.During this interim period, Trusts are implementing manual methods to monitor activity levels. I acknowledge that activity within Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services is a particular area of concern and focus; therefore, ensuring adequate resources in this service remains a key priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what immediate interventions are being considered to reduce the number of children and young people waiting more than nine weeks for a Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service assessment, particularly in the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: BHSCT/SEHSCT –The Trusts continue to work to reduce waiting times within available resources. Additional funding secured through Elective Access monies has enabled contracting with community & voluntary partners to provide interventions to Step 2 children and young people which has significantly reduced waiting times for these services. A waiting list initiative is underway currently to review referrals on the waiting list with overtime clinics being carried out to offer additional CAMHS assessments. Regular review of capacity and demand is carried out alongside timely recruitment to vacant posts to ensure optimisation of available resources.NHSCT – at 23rd July 2025 the numbers of young people waiting for CAMHS Step 3 service is 49 and the current average waiting time for an initial assessment is 30 days. For CAMHS Step 2 services, the numbers of young people are 132, with 41 referrals waiting more than 9 weeks. The current average wait is 45 days with a longest wait of 135 days.  This breach position has been under review and positive progress has been made in recent months on the foot of a number of initiatives including reform in the Children’s Referral Service providing enhanced triage and consistent application of thresholds. This ensures that families are accessing a timely service, most suitable to meet their needs. A number of other initiatives are taking place within the service including the enhancement of the groupwork pathway across CAMHS and the development of virtual resources to support children and families whilst awaiting an initial assessment.   SHSCT – As of May 2025, the Trust had no young people waiting more than nine weeks for CAMHS assessment, up to and including 31st March 2025. On this basis SHSCT are not requiring any immediate interventions with regard to waiting lists for CAMHS, and will continue to keep performance under review and respond accordingly.WHSCT - Whilst the Service meets access targets and ensures prioritisation of referrals that are high risk and urgent, there continue to be ongoing challenges in meeting the routine 9 week access target due to staff vacancies, despite a number of recruitment drives having taken place. Within the context of these challenges, the Service has adopted more creative and responsive ways of working in an attempt to improve timely access to support and to mitigate risks. Service improvement initiatives to address performance have included rolling recruitment drives and realignment of staffing positions to attract a more diverse skilled workforce. CAMHS staff are currently supporting additional new patient clinics to support waiting list initiatives. A number of collaborative quality improvement projects with the wider network of children’s services are currently progressing in an effort to support timely access to appropriate support. Additional non-recurrent funding in December 2024 has supported some additional capacity with a pre-existing CAMHS/ Community provider to off

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what provisions are in place under the Extra Contractual Referral process to facilitate access to specialist inpatient care, including Mother and Baby Unit beds, outside of the north of Ireland for women requiring treatment for postpartum psychosis.
answer_text: Transferring a mother and her baby to a specialist MBU elsewhere in the UK under an Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) has been considered for some cases, and is available when required. However many mothers have expressed that the emotional stress of being separated from their families, friends, and local support networks would outweigh the potential benefits of receiving care further afield.The decision to move a baby from one jurisdiction, such as Northern Ireland, involves significant risks and challenges, particularly in the context of safeguarding concerns and legal constraints.1.	Safeguarding IssuesAny proposed move must ensure that the safety and welfare of the child remain paramount, in line with the principles of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995. If there are existing child protection concerns, relocating the baby may compromise their safety or disrupt established protective arrangements.2.	Court Orders and Legal RestrictionsWhere a court order is in place—such as a Care Order, Emergency Protection Order, or Supervision Order—it may prohibit the removal of the child from the jurisdiction without the explicit permission of the court. 3.	Jurisdictional ComplexityMoving a baby across jurisdictions, even within the UK, may raise complex legal issues under family law, including questions of habitual residence, jurisdiction for future proceedings, and enforceability of child protection measures.4.	Disruption of Care PlanningA move may interfere with ongoing assessments, placement planning, or court-directed interventions, potentially delaying permanency planning and disrupting professional oversight.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) waiting times, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the performance targets or standards that currently apply to CAMHS assessment waiting times; and (ii) which Health and Social Care Trusts are meeting these targets.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the support provided to families of children on Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service waiting lists; and (ii) whether interim psychological or counselling services are made available during extended waits.
answer_text: (i) BHSCT/SEHSCT - In the past 5 years, CAMHS community clinics have experienced longer waits than we would like for our children and young people due to increased demand and complexity of presentation. CAMHS provides a range of supports to young people and their families while they are waiting. Belfast CAMHS has produced booklets for patients and parents/ carers outlining advice and supports available whilst waiting. A duty system is in place in all teams where a clinician is available to discuss any concerns and offer advice. Weekly prioritisation for waiting list allocation is undertaken based on risks and acuity of presentation to ensure any new information is taken into account. The Trust have also contracted with Step 2 Community & Voluntary providers to provide interventions as appropriate to patients which has reduced waiting times significantly for those children requiring Step 2 services.A waiting list initiative is currently underway to review all cases waiting and review appointments are offered if required to those on the waiting list.NHSCT - Upon receipt of a referral to CAMHS, and where clinically indicated, Clinical Referral Co-ordinators contact the family by telephone to discuss the referral in greater detail. During this call, a risk assessment is carried out, bespoke advice and guidance is offered, and if deemed necessary, a safety plan is developed and agreed upon.Families are directed to the Northern Trust CAMHS website for a range of resources. They are also encouraged to access a dedicated podcast series that includes relevant episodes tailored to both CAMHS and other related services, such as those supporting children and young people with ASD. In addition, signposting is provided to appropriate local community and voluntary helplines and services.Where clinically indicated, families are also given information on how to access the CAMHS Crisis Team, which is available both during working hours and out of hours. A waiting list letter of acknowledgement is issued, which includes contact details for the Children’s Referral Service, enabling families to update the team regarding any changes in their child’s presentation and to seek updates on the progress of their referral.SHSCT - CAMHS assess children and adolescents within the timeframe of 9 weeks post referral.  If a child or adolescent is in crisis, the Trust will assess them within 24 hours.WHSCT - The Western Trust Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service is part of a wider network of Children’s Services that operate to provide assessment and appropriate treatment with the aim of meeting a child and /or young person’s emotional, health and wellbeing needs. Children or young people referred for specialist/targeted intervention to CAMHS may have and will continue to have significant support being provided through multi-agency involvement whilst waiting to access CAMHS or indeed following and during care and treatment with CAMHS. This support may be provided through

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional resources or staffing have been allocated to the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service in the last twelve months to address rising demand, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The funding allocated to Trusts over the last 12 months, to address rising demand, was issued specifically with reference to the development of the CAMHS with ID (Intellectual Disability) Service.Recurrent funding has been allocated to:Belfast HSCT £57,518 for Band 6 Nurse for physical health care monitoring for this cohort.Western HSCT £110,890 for Specialist Doctor in Psychiatry to support the development of the CAMHS ID model.  Due to difficulties in recruitment this funding was used in 24/25 to enhance an existing contract with a voluntary provider.Proposed funding for 2025/26 includes South Eastern HSCT £71,000 for a B7 practitioner.Non-recurrent funding was allocated to:Northern HSCT £74,795 to support a small team in the Trust to develop a CAMHS ID service. Proposed funding for 2025/26 includes FYE of this funding.South Eastern HSCT £51,190 for Band 8a Post to lead on developing a CAMHS with ID service. Proposed funding for 2025/26 includes CYE £19,000 to support the work of a consultant paediatrician to develop a regional neurodevelopment pathway.The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health budget. While the funding clearly remains below the level of investment needed, from 2020/21 to 2024/25 funding in CAMHS has increased by 39%. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet this Action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of women diagnosed with postpartum psychosis; and (ii) the number of maternal deaths by suicide linked to serious perinatal mental illness, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Please see below response provided by each TrustTrustNumber of women diagnosed with postpartum psychosisBelfast Trust0 in 2022, 1 in 2023 and 1 in 2024South Eastern Trust South Eastern Trust is limited in reporting on postpartum psychosis. Following a manual trawl of patients who have been in receipt of the Perinatal Mental Health Service since operations began in 2022 - the Trust have identified 30 women with a diagnosis of postpartum psychosis or psychotic features.Northern Trust From Nov 2024 (ENCOMPASS go-live) 7 women have been diagnosed with postpartum psychosis diagnosis – we do not have data before Nov 2024Southern Trust No current mechanism for recording diagnosisWestern Trust The Western Trust does not hold centrally information on the number of women with diagnoses of postpartum psychosis.This information is collected by Public Health Agency (PHA) on behalf of Trusts, and is submitted directly to MBBRACE-UK (Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK).  The PHA do not release data for Northern Ireland separately and it is not published or available to the public, it is for internal use only for service planning and delivery. In addition, PHA guidance on numbers less than 5 means that for most years PHA would be unable to provide any information on these deaths.However MBRRACE-UK have published a number of reports ‘MBRRACE-UK Saving Lives, Improving Mothers' Care – Lessons learned to inform maternity care from the UK and Ireland Confidential Enquiries into Maternal Deaths and Morbidity’, includes surveillance data on women who died during or up to one year after pregnancy in the UK and Ireland.  Due to small numbers and the sensitive nature of these data, MBRRACE-UK only report nationally on three-year cohorts.These reports are available on this link:	  https://www.npeu.ox.ac.uk/mbrrace-uk/reports/maternal-reports

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the status of the business case for a Mother and Baby Unit; and (ii) whether 2030 has been outlined as the expected opening date for the Unit.
answer_text: An earlier independent review identified that the model of the proposed 6/7 bedded Mother and Baby Unit should be that as recommended by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and the location of the Mother and Baby Unit should be the City Hospital site, within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. I have already made it clear to officials within the Department that I wish to see the issue progressed at pace.However, given the importance of the service, urgent work continues to determine the optimum size so that the unit can be future proofed. Belfast Trust are at a very advanced stage in the development of a capital and revenue business case. They have advised that once they receive instruction on the number of beds required, it will take 6 weeks to finalise the business case.Progression of the unit will remain subject to the necessary funding being identified therefore I cannot advise of the expected opening date for the Unit at this time. While the development of a Mother and Baby Unit remains a longer-term strategic objective, my Department has committed to strengthening existing provision in the interim through:Investment in community-based specialist perinatal mental health services.Upskilling of perinatal clinical staff.Implementation of the Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathways and associated guidanceImplementation of crisis intervention pathways and out-of-hours support for women experiencing acute distress.Ongoing engagement with service users and advocacy groups to ensure care remains person-centred and responsive to the needs of mothers and families. Development of a Service User Leaflet to explain the Perinatal Mental Health

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the proposed footway scheme in Hannahstown Village, remains on target to commence during the 2025-26 financial year, as discussed with departmental officials at the meeting held on 26 March 2025.
answer_text: I am aware of the importance of this scheme to the people that live in Hannahstown Village and am committed to making the necessary improvements.As part of the detailed design stage for the new footway, my officials are meeting landowners affected by the scheme to explain our land requirements and agree their accommodation works.The first round of face-to-face meetings with landowners is substantially complete with follow up meetings well underway. The scheme will represent a significant £1.8m investment in the area and due to this substantial value, a separate procurement will need to be undertaken.It is difficult to accurately predict how long it will take to conclude this process as it relies on the Department reaching agreement with individual landowners on the type and scale of work we will undertake, but I expect it to be substantially complete later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the current care pathway for women presenting with postpartum psychosis; and (ii) whether this pathway meets recognised clinical standards or best practice guidelines.
answer_text: (i) Since becoming Health Minister, I have repeatedly stated that improving mental health services, including perinatal mental health, is one of my top priorities. Women who present with symptoms of postpartum psychosis are typically referred urgently to Specialist Community Perinatal Mental health services, often through their GP or maternity services. Once referred, they are assessed by a multidisciplinary mental health team, which may include psychiatrists, psychologist, community psychiatric nurses, perinatal mental health specialist midwives, health visitors, occupational therapists and social workers.A regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust  to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis. Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.However, Northern Ireland does not currently have a dedicated Mother and Baby Unit (MBU), meaning women who require inpatient psychiatric care may be admitted to general psychiatric wards, without their infants.I was delighted to launch the updated Perinatal Mental Health Care Pathway in March 2025 which has integrated the community perinatal mental health teams into the pathway of care. It provides guidance to all health and social care professionals who come into contact with women in the antenatal and postnatal period across all settings, inclusive of primary care.  This includes women presenting with postpartum psychosis for emergency assessment and for specialist assessment.(ii) The current pathway is designed to be consistent with clinical standards set out by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), particularly guideline NG192: Antenatal and postnatal mental health: clinical management and service guidance. However, there are recognised limitations in service provision, notably the absence of an MBU, which is considered best practice for the treatment of postpartum psychosis.The Department of Health acknowledges this gap and remains committed to improving perinatal mental health services. Work is ongoing to assess the feasibility of establishing a regional MBU and to enhance community-based specialist services, in line with national standards and the recommendations of the Royal College of Psychiatrists and other expert bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the perinatal mental health training currently being delivered across each Health and Social Care Trust, including (i) whether this training includes postpartum psychosis; (ii) the number of staff trained, broken down by professional group, including GPs, GP receptionists, Emergency Department staff, and Ambulance Service personnel; and (iii) the organisations responsible for delivering the training.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what actions are being taken to deliver his Department’s commitment, as set out in the Cancer Strategy 2022-2023, to review current cancer waiting time targets to ensure equity across the patient pathway; (ii) the projected timeline for the introduction of a 28-day standard from first referral for suspected cancer to confirmation of a cancer diagnosis; and (iii) whether his Department intends to retain specific and ambitious targets for breast cancer as part of this review.
answer_text: Action 14 of the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 refers to a review of current cancer targets to ensure equity of access across the pathway. In addition to this, action 13 refers to the introduction of a 28-day standard from first referral for suspected cancer.Officials in my Department will be undertaking a review of the current cancer targets in 2025, This review will consider the introduction of a 28-day standard, along with the current targets for breast cancer.Further details on the potential rollout of the 28-day standard will be provided following completion of this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the role of artificial intelligence in patient selection for thrombectomy; and (ii) any plans to expand its use.
answer_text: (i) Artificial intelligence (AI) in stroke imaging supports patient selection for thrombectomy in seven of the eight stroke receiving units by providing:faster data to support clinicians in diagnosis and treatment decisions (time is brain). an electronic communication platform, including image sharing, between RVH (hub) and DGH (spokes), reducing time to decision on thrombectomy transferstroke hospitals outside RVH access to CT Perfusion (CTP) imaging for the first time. CTP imaging is required in patient assessment against extended criteria for thrombectomy, in line with recently updated guidance.(ii) AI will be rolled out in the final stroke receiving unit in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what actions are being taken to deliver a 24/7 thrombectomy service for acute stroke patients (ii) the projected timeline for implementation; (iii) the current provision of thrombectomy services for acute stroke patients, including operational hours and the annual number of procedures performed; and (iv) to detail what funding has been allocated for thrombectomy services in each of the last three years.

To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to address workforce requirements, including interventional neuroradiology, stroke and anaesthetic staff necessary for a 24/7 thrombectomy service.
answer_text: A costed model to expand thrombectomy services to 24/7 is currently being developed and includes a range of delivery options and workforce requirements. The majority of the options include the need for substantial investment and the recruitment of specialist staff such as interventional neuroradiology, stroke and anaesthetic staff, within a limited workforce pool.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what engagement has taken place with the regional Stroke Network and clinical leads regarding the development of a 24/7 thrombectomy service; and (ii) how does he plan to mitigate the impact of any delays in implementing a 24/7 thrombectomy service on patient outcomes.
answer_text: (i) A number of meetings have taken place between my representatives, members of the regional Stroke Network and Stroke Clinical Leads as part of the development of a costed model for a 24/7 thrombectomy service. These engagements commenced a number of years ago and have continued regularly throughout the planning process. They have focused on agreeing baseline staffing levels, identifying existing and future investment requirements, outlining workforce needs, and discussing the key challenges associated with service expansion. This work has been undertaken collaboratively to ensure the model is clinically informed and regionally appropriate.(ii) While the implementation of a 24/7 thrombectomy service remains constrained by current budgetary pressures, my department is committed to minimising the impact of any delays on patient outcomes.We continue to maximise the effectiveness of the existing thrombectomy service within its current operational hours. In parallel, we have published the Reshaping Stroke Care – Stroke Action Plan, which sets out priority actions to improve stroke care more broadly.As part of this, we have introduced a revised thrombectomy repatriation protocol to support the flow of patients in and out of the service. In addition, Artificial Intelligence (AI) in stroke imaging, to support rapid stroke diagnosis and decision making has been implemented in seven stroke receiving units, with rollout underway in the remaining unit. These improvements are designed to enhance speed of diagnosis, access to treatment, and overall outcomes for stroke patients.While a fully 24/7 thrombectomy service is not yet in place, these measures are actively supporting better patient care during the current operating hours and laying the groundwork for future expansion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress of the Rapid Drug Testing model at Queen's University Belfast.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Go Succeed service.
answer_text: Since its launch in November 2023, Go Succeed has established itself as a key part of our economic infrastructure and has had an ongoing positive impact on our local entrepreneurs and businesses.  It acts as an important lever to delivering my priorities of productivity and regional balance, by providing support across all areas of the north.Underlining this importance, Go Succeed figures show that over 6,000 entrepreneurs were supported to get started across the north. Over 4,365 existing businesses have been supported on how to grow their business, and 389 social enterprises were supported to start or grow.Currently around £5.5m in grant funding has been awarded and nearly 15,000 entrepreneurs and business have attended the 903 masterclasses delivered.Last year my predecessor and I, in my former capacity as Finance Minister, met with the British Government to press the case for continued funding for Go Succeed.  I continue to be committed to working proactively with officials to push for future funding.Therefore, I am pleased that £9.2m of extended funding until March 2026 has been secured through the Shared Prosperity Fund.  This is a reduction from previous funding and I continue to press for a positive announcement regarding future funding in Wednesday’s Spending Review.  I am also delighted at the recent news on the new self-employment programme for people with disabilities – a collaboration that includes Go Succeed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether cardiovascular health will be included as a standalone priority in the Women’s Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The Women’s Health Action Plan will consider a broad range of health issues faced by women throughout their life course.Through ongoing dialogue with the British Heart Foundation and others I am informed about the under-awareness, under-diagnosis and under-treatment of cardiovascular disease among women.  I recognise that there are particular challenges in terms of improving awareness of cardiovascular risk factors among women and girls, and addressing any differences in terms of diagnosis and treatment.  The importance of focussing on heart health was also raised during the recent online Women’s Health stakeholder engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals.  My officials are now considering the input received to help inform the priorities in the action plan including how to improve education and awareness on a range of women’s health issues and risk factors.In the meantime, as one of the guiding principles of the Northern Ireland Cardiac Network is to work to ensure equity of access to high quality cardiac care for all of the population, my Department will continue to work closely with the Cardiac Network to consider any improvements that could be applied to current diagnostic and treatment pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the next tranche of part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. Since 2020/21, the Department has delivered 2 tranches of part-time 20mph speed limits at 216 schools, an investment of £4m, bringing the total number of schools with part-time 20mph speed limits to 233.A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site.  The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the 2025/26 financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies and obviously considering the safety of introducing such measures. Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools is included within my budget plans for the 2025/26 financial year and I intend to make an announcement on plans for the next tranche in the coming monthsI would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits.To ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits here, I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.
