question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the independent review into the level of need, undertaken by Professor Holtham.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the remedial work required at the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: Belfast Trust have recently undertaken exploratory works in two bedrooms within the Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre to determine the full extent of the remediation works required and any other factors contributing to the high levels of moisture and deterioration of the building.A consultant who was not involved with the original project has been commissioned by the Trust to monitor the works on site, and to provide a report assessing any defects that may be uncovered and recommending proposed remediation works.  This report will provide estimated costs and programme for the works.The exploratory works have uncovered areas of concern including high levels of damp within the subfloor.  The Trust await assessment from the consultant as to whether the damp in the sub-floor is exclusively a result of the pinhole leaking in the pipework that I have previously referenced, or whether there is evidence of water ingress from another source.Until the report is finalised I am unable to outline the remedial works required.  I have been advised by the Belfast Trust that the final report is expected within three weeks.In closing I can confirm that I expect all necessary measures to be taken to mitigate the impact of any remedial works required on the staff and patients who occupy this building.  I do not wish to speculate as to what measures may be taken at this stage as I do not wish to add to the concerns of patients and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the sectoral study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland, commissioned by his Department.
To ask the Minister of Health how much an extra-contractual referral costs for a patient to receive mesh services in Britain.
answer_text: The Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process enables local Trust consultants to seek funding approval to transfer a named patient to a provider outside Northern Ireland.  This applies in cases where there is requirement for assessment or treatment which the consultant considers necessary, but is not available in local health and social care facilities.  This may be either because the treatment needed is of a specialist nature, or because there is a clinical reason why the local service is not appropriate. Costs associated with NHS treatment, is provided through the National Tariff Scheme and associated HRG codes. These are codes specific to the treatment plan applied to individual patients’, and applied at a national level. As SPPG monitor the submission and approvals of tertiary referral requests only, we do not hold associated costs, for specific treatments, currently.Associated travel costs, to send patients outside NI for treatment, will vary depending on the method of transport required, time of travel, length of stay in GB and related accommodation costs. The SPPG will fund travel and reimbursement, associated patient travel expenses, in line with SPPG travel policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of money that has been spent on the mesh clinic in Belfast, in addition to the existing funding for urogynaecological services, since its inception in 2018; (ii) how this money has been spent in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In September 2023, my Department confirmed that a recurrent allocation of £530,000 was now being made available to support the provision of a vaginal mesh service in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, additional to the existing gynaecology service.  This commissioned service is fully funded to deliver: 3 regional multispecialty multidisciplinary outpatient clinics per month. 2 additional regional scan clinics per month (4 patients per clinic); 2 additional regional multidisciplinary pain management clinics per month (2 patients per clinic); 1 additional multispecialty inpatient full day operating list (2-3 patients per list) per month and 108 bed days per year; 2 regional virtual multispecialty multidisciplinary meetings with other Trusts per month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of mesh removal surgeries, which remove the full implant, that have taken place since 2022.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust have confirmed that as of 4 March 2025 there have been 31 total removals (10 TVT and 21 TVT-O).There have also been 6 TVT partial removals, 4 partial TVT-O removals and 6 removals from organs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department is supporting small community groups with the cost of childcare; and (ii) whether he has any plans to make additional funding available for local community creches.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is leading on a scoping exercise  to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements of the childcare sector, including voluntary and community providers.This work is due to conclude in March 2025 and will inform proposals within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, which I intend to bring forward to the Executive in autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of funding allocated to Shared Lives for Older People in the last three years; (ii) the number of older people that have availed of this service in the last three years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (iii) the number of carers for the service that have been recruited and trained to date.
answer_text: The older people’s shared lives service in NI, is commissioned regionally by the Department of Health and provided by Age NI. The Shared Lives Service for Older People commenced on the 9th January 2023.  From January 2023 to end of December 2024, £311,472 has been allocated.There are 10 individuals matched with Shared Lives Carers; 6 placements in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and 4 placements in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts.  Individuals in the other 3 Health and Social Care Trust are currently being assessed to become Shared Lives Carers. There are 16 Shared Lives Carers recruited and trained to date.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Strategy for Victims and Survivors of the Troubles/Conflict 2024-2034.
answer_text: We are committed to meeting the needs of victims and survivors and we were pleased that the Executive endorsed the new Strategy in October 2024. It marks a significant step forward in support for victims and survivors. Work is ongoing with our co-design partners, the Commission for Victims and Survivors and the Victims and Survivors Service, to develop specific operational action plans and identify resources required to take forward the commitments outlined in the Strategy.  Our approach continues to be built on collaborative design, with victims and survivors at the heart of the process. Our initial focus is the design and implementation of new VSS funding programmes, which we expect to have in place by 1 April 2026. This is a central activity needed to implement many of the commitments in the Strategy. We are keen to ensure that services can be flexible so that they are able to meet the changing needs of victims and survivors. Medium and longer-term actions will include the roll out of trauma-informed practice and education to wider society and raising awareness of the needs of victims and survivors with other service providers, across Government and in other jurisdictions.To ensure we maintain and develop our understanding of the current and changing needs of victims and survivors there will also be further research, led by the Commission for Victims and Survivors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the new Maternity Hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital will open; and (ii) when construction work will commence on the new Children's Hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital.
answer_text: (i) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are not yet in a position to confirm when the new Maternity Hospital will open.  As previously reported to the Health Committee, the Trust are currently sampling the existing water system. Upon receipt of these sampling results the Trust will be able to determine the level of remediation works required to the water systems.  Once this remediation decision has been made, during April 2025, the Trust will be able to provide a programme for the opening of the new maternity hospital. (ii) Construction work commenced on the new Children's Hospital on 3 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the post of Chief Psychologist has been filled.
answer_text: An individual has been identified for the post of Chief Psychological Professions Officer following interviews at end January 2025. Details of the appointment are currently being finalised and an announcement will be made in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when construction works will commence on the mother and baby unit at the Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: Financial projections in the Department of Healths capital plan, currently under review, indicates that construction for the Regional Perinatal Mental Health Mother and Baby Unit at Belfast City Hospital is estimated to start during the 2026/2027 financial year.The project is at an early stage, with work ongoing by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to finalise an Outline Business Case, in which cost and time projections will be further refined.  Programme projections at this stage are subject to change, and will be impacted by the date of submission, subsequent approvals process, funding availability and design team input.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when suicide prevention barriers will be erected on bridges over the Westlink in Belfast.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the significance of the provision of these measures to the Divis and Clifton Street Bridges over Westlink. I can confirm that my officials are currently finalising the detailed design, contract documents and business case for this scheme at an estimated investment of £1.8m. Although budgets for next year are not known, should funding be available, I would hope that measures could be in place by early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the Women's Health Action Plan (WHAP) will take into account the recommendations made in the October 2024 Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care report; (ii) when the WHAP will be completed; and (iii) what funding will be made available for the WHAP.
answer_text: One of the aims of the Women’s Health Action Plan is to highlight all of the work currently underway within the Department and in Health and Social Care in relation to improving health outcomes for women and girls.  This will include the ongoing programme of work to improve maternity and neonatal safety, of which the Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care in Northern Ireland report is one particular strand. Work to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan is ongoing.  My Department recently hosted a number of online engagement events with women’s representative groups and healthcare professionals and will be reviewing the input received over the coming weeks to help inform the priorities in the action plan. I hope to be in a position to provide a timescale for publishing the plan thereafter.Due to the severe financial constraints facing my Department there is currently no new additional funding available for the Women’s Health Action Plan.  The challenge is therefore to focus on what can be achieved within current resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any progress has been made on securing an all-island solution to the current lack of a paediatric pathology service; and (ii) whether the Minister for Health in the South of Ireland has confirmed that they are willing to extend their paediatric pathology service with constituents in the North of Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the accommodation works for the new footway scheme in Hannahstown village, West Belfast.
answer_text: As part of the detailed design stage for the new footway in Hannahstown Village, my officials are currently meeting landowners who will be affected by the scheme to explain land requirements and agree accommodation works. Land is required from 24 landowners and 3 departmental officials have been allocated to complete this task. The first round of face-to-face meetings with landowners is substantially complete and the second round of follow up meetings is now underway. It is difficult to accurately predict how long it will take to conclude this process as it relies on my officials reaching agreement with individual landowners on the type and extent of work to be undertaken, but I hope it will be substantially complete later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has the power to set up its own redress scheme for those injured by vaginal mesh.
answer_text: The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by Sodium Valproate and Pelvic Mesh implants in the “Hughes Report”, which was published on 7 February 2024. Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  The three Devolved Administrations are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) and a UK Government decision on redress has not yet been taken.It is unfortunate that those who have been harmed by both Pelvic Mesh and Sodium Valproate have had to wait so long on clarity around the way forward in response to the Hughes Report.I wrote to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health and Mental Health at the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and have sought assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations will take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions. Baroness Merron responded that the UK Government is carefully considering the report before coming to a decision. She recognises that any response by the UK Government is likely to have implications for the Devolved Administrations and is keen to work closely with officials across the UK in considering the report’s recommendations.  She has provided assurance that my officials and I will be engaged in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government Response.The approach to redress will be informed by the UK Government position, which is not yet determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) any financial and psychological support offered to victims of the sodium valporate scandal; (ii) the number of women and children that have been affected; and (iii) whether his Department will consider redress payments to those affected.
answer_text: (i) The Patient Safety Commissioner for England, Dr Henrietta Hughes, made recommendations on redress for those who were harmed by Sodium Valproate and Pelvic Mesh implants in the “Hughes Report”, which was published on 7 February 2024. Whilst Dr Hughes’ remit was specifically for England, it is acknowledged that patients from across the UK have been affected by these issues.  The three Devolved Administrations are awaiting consideration and assessment from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) and a UK Government decision on redress has not yet been taken.It is unfortunate that those who have been harmed by both Pelvic Mesh and Sodium Valproate have had to wait so long on clarity around the way forward in response to the Hughes Report.All who have suffered harm would be able to avail of HSC services and relevant social security benefits.(ii) The Department established a Data Scoping Group in August 2024, to source data currently available to support the identification of Northern Ireland patients harmed by pelvic mesh and the medicine sodium valproate and to identify any data gaps. The Data Scoping Group (DSG) will also consider the UKG response to the Hughes Report, when available. (iii) I wrote to Baroness Merron, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Patient Safety, Women’s Health and Mental Health at the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), requesting an update on the consideration of the Hughes Report recommendations and have sought assurances that consultation with the Devolved Administrations will take place in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government decisions. Baroness Merron responded that the UK Government is carefully considering the report before coming to a decision. She recognises that any response by the UK Government is likely to have implications for the Devolved Administrations and is keen to work closely with officials across the UK in considering the report’s recommendations.  She has provided assurance that my officials and I will be engaged in a timely manner and in advance of any UK Government Response.The approach to redress will be informed by the UK Government position, which is not yet determined.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his plans to deliver Community Wealth Building.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to include stricter sentencing for drug-related offences in her Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: Depending on the Class of drug concerned, possession of a controlled drug carries a possible maximum prison sentence of up to 7 years.  Offences of production, supply and possession with intent to supply command possible maximum sentences of 14 years’ imprisonment or life.  These are significant maxima, to which I have no plans to bring forward any changes at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether discussions have been ongoing in relation to attaching an aggravated factor on the supplying of illegal drugs on the streets.
answer_text: The maximum penalties available for the offence of supply of controlled drugs are currently 14 years’ imprisonment for Class B or Class C drugs and life imprisonment for Class A drugs. Given significant penalties are already available, the reasons for creating a statutory aggravating factor, and the nature of any such aggravator, would need to be fully understood and explored in depth with police, prosecutors and the courts to gather views on its workability.   Due to limited resources and competing priorities there has not yet been an opportunity to engage with external agencies in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the New Script for Mental Health's Give 5: Steps to a Wellbeing Rights Framework; and (ii) whether his Department will commit to integrating this framework into the Health and Social Care Trusts' approach to mental health.
answer_text: I am aware that the network Participation and the Practice of Rights (PPR) recently launched its five steps approach to mental health entitled ‘Give 5: Steps to a Wellbeing Rights based Framework’, which sets out what it believes government and organisations can do to better tackle the mental health crisis.  Whilst there are no current plans for the HSC Trusts to formally adopt PPR’s Framework, it should be noted that the themes are broadly aligned with the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, which sets out the future strategic direction of mental health services for Northern Ireland.  The Strategy, which includes 35 actions set out under three overarching themes, is person-centred and takes a whole life and whole system approach.  The key aim of the Strategy is to ensure long-term improved outcomes for the mental health of the population, recognising that a person’s mental health is shaped by a range of social, economic, cultural and environmental factors and notes the need for a joined up approach across government, sectors and the whole of society to implement existing policies designed to address deprivation, poverty, loneliness and social cohesion issues, and other social determinants of mental ill health.Furthermore, it notes that the vision for Northern Ireland is a society which promotes emotional wellbeing and positive mental health for everyone with a lifespan approach, which supports recovery, and seeks to reduce stigma and mental health inequalities. Full implementation of the Strategy, when funding permits, will create a system that ensures consistency and equity of access to services, so that the individual and their needs are placed right at the centre, respecting diversity, equality and human rights, and ensuring people have access to the most appropriate, high-quality help and treatment at the right time, and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any plans to provide Bunscoil Phobal Feirste Primary School with a permanent Specialist Provision in a Mainstream School (SPiM) solution by developing more bespoke buildings or classrooms and allow the school to retrieve back accommodation they initially conceded for the SPiM to be built; and (ii) when a permanent solution will be built.
answer_text: In 2023 internal reconfiguration works were completed to three classrooms at Bunscoil Phobal Feirste to facilitate the opening of two SPiM classes for September 2023.  A 144m2 extension to the school building was also delivered to provide multi-purpose and small group support space for pupils with Special Educational Needs (SEN).Whilst there are currently no planned minor or SEN capital works to deliver additional accommodation at the school, I have asked the head of my SEN capital investment programme team to arrange a visit to the school to discuss its accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether Applied Behavioural Analysis treatment is used for those with complex behaviours and autism; (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts that provide this service; (iii) the amount of funding that goes towards providing this service; and (iv) any discussions that have taken place with the Department of Education to use this treatment in schools.
answer_text: My Department recognises that there are a number of interventions for autism, including applied behaviour analysis (ABA), but does not advocate for a particular approach. This is a decision for clinicians to make, based on individually assessed needs and good practice evidence, such as NICE guidance.All Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT) have confirmed that they do not provide or promote the use of applied behavioural analysis as a treatment for those with complex behaviours and autism. All HSCTs have also confirmed that they do spend any funding on ABA in Trust nor do they fund any outside organisation to in relation to ABA.  No discussions have taken place between my Department and the Department of Education (DE) to use this intervention in schools. Nor have any discussions between individual Trusts and DE taken place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has given any consideration to the introduction of Applied Behaviour Analysis (i) treatments; and (ii) teachers in our schools.
answer_text: Responsibility for legislation, strategy and policy in relation to Autism sits with the Department of Health (DoH).  My department does not have any role in the introduction of any (i) treatments.(ii)	The provision of supports for autistic children and young people, their families, teachers and support staff in our schools are operational matters for the Education Authority (EA) and Middletown Centre for Autism.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the progress made on the introduction of a regional mental health crisis service; and (ii) the cost for introducing this service.
answer_text: Implementing the new Regional Crisis Service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under Actions 12 and 27. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed Regional Mental Health Crisis Service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 Future State work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop took place on the 8th March 2024 with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services. The Workshop outcomes helped progress the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, including pathways and access to crisis emergency mental health.A subsequent Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Workshop took place on 11th October 2024. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway was presented to stakeholders. Stakeholders provided feedback on the draft pathway, discussed gaps within services and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Crisis Service model. The workshop feedback is currently being reviewed and will be presented at the December 2024 Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Strategic Implementation Group. The Workshop outcomes will help progress the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, including pathways and access to crisis emergency mental health. My Department officials continue to engage with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services to develop the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Model.(ii)To date, my Department has been using existing funding to progress implementation of the Mental Health Strategy. While this funding allows the continuation of work on the Strategy, given the extremely challenging budgetary situation, it does not match the levels of funding identified in the Strategy’s Funding Plan. The development of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service will involve the identification of funding. My officials are working alongside relevant stakeholders on the economic analysis of introducing a range of aspects of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for Northern Ireland. In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and will be in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the care pathway in place for people with myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME) or chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS); (ii) whether there are dedicated consultants or clinics to treat ME and CFS in the health service; and (iii) whether GPs have been provided with training on how to treat ME and CFS following the review of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines in 2021.
answer_text: (i) Patients who have symptoms of ME/CFS and have contacted their GP should be offered treatment for their symptoms in line with NICE guidelines.  The GP may refer the patient to specialists to exclude other potential underlying causes and may refer the patient for therapeutic interventions.Although the current arrangements for the provision of ME/CFS services in Health and Social Care Trusts varies in each Trust; support includes occupational therapy led ME/CFS programme, self-management programmes and support and symptom management through core services that best meet the individual’s needs.(ii) There are currently no designated medical practitioners working with this patient group. The service model for myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME)/chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS) that was previously agreed with voluntary organisations representing people living with ME/CFS, made provision for a medical lead with a specialist interest in ME/CFS. Within this service model the medical lead would provide guidance and evidence-based leadership to local healthcare teams and GPs to provide appropriate treatments.  To date, Belfast Trust has been unable to fill this post.   (iii) In Northern Ireland, ME/CFS patients are typically managed through Primary and Community Care services. Training for General Practitioners (GPs) in the management of people with ME/CFS, is currently included in the Membership of the Royal College of General Practitioners (MRCGP) curriculum. Practice teams attend GP led education sessions, arranged and delivered via the Federation Support Units. GPNI is another educational platform available to GPs, which hosts a weekly one hour zoom meeting to deliver education to GPs and their teams. A GPNI education session has been scheduled for March 2025 to raise awareness of this condition and help improve the knowledge of the next generation of healthcare professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the social enterprise co-design group.
answer_text: Question 12 – AQO 1120/22-27I met with the Social Enterprise Co-Design Group at the end of September when the initial actions for the Social Enterprise Action Plan were presented to me and discussed. The full action plan is currently being finalised and I look forward to launching it during Social Enterprise week later this month. Work has already begun to implement the group’s initial actions to strengthen the commercial base of social enterprises. Champions will be appointed within Invest NI, Intertrade Ireland and local councils, to assist social enterprises navigate the support available already. Improved opportunities arising from commissioning is being explored by my officials and those in Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget 2024.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their plans to develop a Racial Equality Strategy post-2025.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline his Department's investment in Irish-medium schools in West Belfast over the last three years, to support children with special educational needs.
answer_text: There are seven Irishmedium schools in the West Belfast constituency.  735 of the 2,141 pupils who attend these schools are registered as having special educational needs and 158 of those pupils have a statement of special educational needs.Total investment to support children with special educational needs is difficult to disaggregate.A total of £28.28 million was allocated via the Common Funding Formula to the seven schools over the last three years.  All pupils within these schools benefit from the school budget and expenditure on pupils with special educational needs cannot be disaggregated from it.Total expenditure on the Education Authority’s Special Educational Needs Pupil Support Services was £51.9 million across the three years.  All schools in receipt of Pupil Support Services benefit from this investment and it cannot be disaggregated for individual schools.The 158 children and young people with a statement of special educational needs receive individualised special educational needs support as set out in their statement and the Education Authority has confirmed a spend of £4.6m on this support over the last three years.   There are currently seven specialist education classes across three Irish-medium schools in West Belfast, all of which have been established in the last three years.  Investment in these seven classes over the last three years has totalled £802k capital and £899k recurrent funding.A total of £341k was also allocated to the seven schools during this period to assist with  the new special educational needs framework which will see the implementation of the Special Educational Needs Act and the associated new Code of Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) whether the review of Protect Life 2 is complete; and (ii) when the revised plan will be published.
answer_text: The Review of the Protect Life 2 Action Plan undertaken by independent consultants is complete and was published on 27 September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Professor Mary Renfrew’s report into maternity services will be published.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is exploring the use of psychological autopsies following a death by suicide.
answer_text: My Department is not exploring the use of psychological autopsies following a death by suicide.The Serious Adverse Incident (SAI) Redesign team has assessed the potential applicability of the Psychological Autopsy tool to learning reviews. The assessment is that the tool may not be the most appropriate methodology or tool to identify new learning leading to service improvement following an incident.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review in Tier 4 residential rehabilitation services will be published.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for the Department of Health, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental actions and support.Outcome C of the Substance Use Strategy is that “People have Access to High Quality Treatment and Support Services.”  To contribute to and deliver this Outcome, a ‘Review of Regional Tier 4a and Tier 4b Addiction Services in Northern Ireland’ was commissioned by the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) in 2023. This work sits within the wider Strategic Commissioning and Implementation Plan 2024-2028 as led by the SPPG and PHA.I am pleased to advise that the initial draft report has been received and being reviewed by Officials. The key findings and recommendations have recently been shared with Tier 4 service providers including current Community and Voluntary Sector providers and HSCTs. It is my intention that the recommendations made within the report will be subject to a 12-week public consultation process which will begin in Autumn 2024. This will allow for all interested citizens to comment on the proposed vision for these services and the actions required to achieve the recommendations in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a breakdown of how many full mesh removals have been carried out by the Mesh Centre in Belfast.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Mesh Centre at the Belfast City Hospital was established as part of the response to the work of the NI Vaginal Mesh Review that commenced in September 2017. Over the last 5 years (up to April 2024), the Northern Ireland Mesh Centre have completed 38 mesh removal surgeries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department will carry out a review of the 111 helpline initiative currently active in England, particularly its recent expansion to include mental health services; and (ii) whether he will assess its effectiveness and consider implementing a similar service, ensuring that it would complement existing resources such as Lifeline.
answer_text: The Urgent and Emergency Care Review was published in 2022, with one of the strategic priorities being the introduction of local, and ultimately regional, Phone First services, akin to the 111 telephone services provided in other areas of the UK.Local Phone First services are now available in all Trust areas to provide initial advice and triage for those people considering attending urgent care services.  While these services currently operate under different telephone numbers, my Department is also exploring the possibility of introducing a regional HSC111 telephone triage service, which will bring these local services under one, easy to remember telephone number, subject to business case development and funding availability. Officials have been engaging with counterparts in England and Scotland to review how their services currently operate, including the introduction of a mental health hub as part of any new service.  This hub would provide mental health assessment and support, including signposting callers to other relevant mental health services. Additionally, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service has been testing a ‘hear and treat’ mental health service as part of its 999 response. The service is currently operational during peak times, with mental health professionals available in ambulance control from 6pm to 2am on Friday to Sunday evenings. During the trial phase, the service has provided care to almost 100 patients, and it aims to expand its coverage to all Trust areas in the coming months. So far, the mental health professionals have been successful in treating more than one-third of callers over the telephone, and they have directly referred more than one-quarter to other services within the Health and Social Care system or in the community.  This service offers more suitable alternatives than transporting patients with mental health issues to busy Emergency Departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
To ask the Minister of Finance when the June Monitoring Round will take place.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how he plans to contribute to the overarching target of 80 per cent of energy coming from renewable sources by 2030.
answer_text: As Minister, I am committed to making a positive contribution and I recognise that our planning system has an important supporting role.  	I want to ensure that our planning system operates efficiently and effectively within an appropriate legislative and policy framework. 	Last year, changes to planning legislation to expand permitted development rights for the installation of microgeneration equipment, such as heat pumps were brought forward.	Later this year, I expect to publish revised regional planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy to inform plan making and decision taking within the wider context for energy and climate change. 	While there is no single solution that will address the multiple and complex issues that are impacting the planning system here, I also believe that the progress that is being made through my Department’s Planning Improvement Programme will similarly help deliver positive outcomes.	Planning is, however, a collective issue and the whole system and key parties to delivering it, must be properly funded and resourced in terms of their capacity and capability.	More widely, my Department is working collaboratively with DoF, DfE, and DAERA to progress initiatives that will assist in reducing NI Water’s dependency on fossil fuel energy sources and help reach net carbon zero goals.	NI Water’s ‘Power of Water’ report provides an ambitious proposal in terms of the role NI Water could play as a catalyst for decarbonising our energy supply. 	My Department has assisted NI Water in the first steps in that journey, for example by supporting the installation of solar farms and an associated battery energy storage project. NI Water is also progressing an innovative Electrolyser project to improve wastewater processing and provide green energy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children and adolescents on a waiting list for (i) the Drug and Alcohol Mental Health Service; and ii) the Eating Disorder Youth Service.
answer_text: At 30th April 2024 i) fewer than 5 children and young people were on a waiting list for the Drug and Alcohol Mental Health Service; and ii) 18 children and young people were on a waiting list for CAMHS Eating Disorder services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be a budget allocated to child and adolescent psychotherapy.
answer_text: My Department undertook a mental health workforce review as part of the Mental Health Strategy. The future role of child and adolescent psychotherapy was recognised within this review.  The current budget position has not enabled a budget to be allocated to child and adolescent psychotherapy at this time. This position will be reviewed in light of new and emerging roles identified within the workforce review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on progress that has been made with the new children’s hospital; (ii) whether (a) planning; and (b) funding is in place; and (iii) to detail the delivery timeframe.
answer_text: The Full Business Case (FBC) for the New Children’s Hospital is currently under review. DoH officials are working closely with the Belfast Trust and DoF to improve the robustness of the FBC and secure approval before the end of June 2024 so that the contract can be awarded and construction works can commence.(iia)	The New Children’s Hospital and demolition of Bostock House planning application was submitted on 21st August 2018 and approved on 18th April 2019. This approval is not subject to expiry as the approved works have commenced.  (iib)	As the New Children’s Hospital is an Executive flagship project, it will be prioritised and funded from the Department’s capital budget through to completion.  Whilst it is not possible to identify and secure revenue funding so far in advance of the completion of the project, it is expected that revenue funding to support the running and additional staffing of the New Childrens Hospital will be prioritised from future budget settlements. The New Children’s Hospital is projected to commence on site in early 2025 subject to Full Business Case approval in summer 2024. The building works are programmed for a period of 4.5 years to complete in summer 2029. A commissioning phase will follow with the hospital expected to be operational by Q4 2029/30.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on a business case for Translink's project to install a number of new bus shelters across the public transport network.
answer_text: I recognise that bus shelters are an integral part of the public transport service and provide passengers with shelter and safety from the elements whilst waiting for bus services. I know that Translink regularly receive requests for the provision of new bus shelters at stops throughout the province from passenger focus groups, members of the public, community and elected representatives. There is also a need to replace/refurbish the existing shelter in order to maintain levels of condition.My officials are currently considering a business case from Translink seeking to replace/refurbish existing bus shelters as well as installing a number of new bus shelters across the public transport network. This work will be progressed in the coming period subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on transformation funding.
answer_text: It has been clear for too long that the level of funding available for public services is not sufficient, but also that we must transform how we provide public services to deliver better for citizens. Public services across many sectors are in critical need of investment and reform to ensure the quality and efficiency of service citizens quite rightly expect. Transformation is also essential if the Executive is to put its finances on a sustainable footing.The financial package for the restored Executive includes £235m of ring-fenced funding for Public Sector Transformation. This provides £47m each year, over a 5-year period. The Executive agreed on 9 May to my proposed approach for making quick progress on the use of this important funding. I have now established, and appointed members to, an interim Public Sector Transformation Board.The interim Board will be chaired by the Head of the Civil Service, Jayne Brady, and include Professor Frances Ruane, an independent Non-Executive member of the NICS Board, and who was previously Director of the Economic and Social Research Institute in Dublin. Julie Harrison, NIO Permanent Secretary, will also join the interim Board having been nominated by the Secretary of State.  The interim Board is working at pace and has issued a call for Departments to submit transformation proposals. Once assessed, the interim Board will make recommendations on those projects that could be supported by this funding.I look forward to considering these and making progress on the transformation that is so urgently needed in our public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he is taking to reduce waiting times in (i) clinical psychology; and (ii) psychological therapy services.
answer_text: All Trust’s Psychological Services are experiencing significant waiting list pressures within a number of services. Trusts have highlighted waiting list pressures are being experienced for a number of reasons, including:•	Demand for assessment and treatment from services vastly outweighing capacity. •	Additional investment in psychological therapy services would be required to meet this need.•	Vacancies within services and challenges filling vacant posts. •	Investment in additional training places would be required to meet the level of need.•	Increased referrals for those with more complex needs, leading to longer treatment tails which reduces ‘flow’ through services.In the absence of any additional funding, Trusts have attempted a number of measures to reduce waiting times. These include:•	Working to fill vacancies in a timely manner.•	Contributing to plans for the development of an alternative workforce, such as a Clinical Associate Psychologist (CAP) training programme within NI.•	Reviewing Integrated Elective Access Protocols (IEAP’s) across all services in line with Department of Health September 2023 update.•	Reviewing service operational policies to increase efficiency, including referral triage processes, staff job plans and caseload review processes.•	Close scrutiny and management of waiting lists (including validation exercises and effective communication with those who are waiting).•	Productivity measures in the Psychological Services to reduce DNA’s for review appointments.•	The development of early intervention approaches •	The provision of group workshop/interventions in a timely fashion, in order to prioritise those with more complex needs waiting for 1:1 therapy.•	The use of QI methodology for service improvement projects and management of waiting lists.Whilst all the actions above have improved the efficiency and effectiveness of the services provided, additional investment will be required to address the significant demand and capacity gaps in order to make meaningful reductions in the waiting times for both Clinical Psychology and Psychological Therapy services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will he make an assessment of the impact of support funds for families with children in long-term hospital stays which operate in Britain.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made an assessment of the cost incurred by families with sick children needing care.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider the establishment of a dedicated young patient’s fund to financially support families with children who have to attend regular hospital-based treatments and reviews.
answer_text: Help with travel costs associated with medical treatment is provided via the Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme, which also provides financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens. The scheme is open to individuals and their dependents, primarily on low incomes. The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefit recipients automatically passport on to the scheme. Eligibility in terms of age is determined by the eligibility rules for benefits Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of the update is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department for Communities (DfC).The commencement of a wider review of the provisions within these Regulations and associated HwHC scheme will be considered once this urgent work is completed and in line with available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will make an assessment of the detrimental mental health impact that financial pressure has on caregivers of sick children, on top of the stress brought on by the emotional and practical care of an ill child.
answer_text: I understand fully how challenging and stressful it can be for families trying to care for their sick child, whilst also having to manage the additional financial pressures which can arise. To help with those challenges there are existing schemes in place in Northern Ireland to provide assistance to children and families who need access to healthcare. The Help with Healthcare Costs Scheme provides individuals and their dependants, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lenses, and travel costs for health and social care treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The eligibility criteria are set out in legislation and certain benefits automatically passport on to the scheme.In addition, should a child be sent outside of Northern Ireland to access specialist care and treatment, costs will be met for both parents to travel with the child. On Wednesday 22 May, I met with the Co-Chairs of the Northern Ireland Children’s Health Coalition to discuss these issues. I was also delighted to co-sponsor the launch of their document ‘The Hidden Cost of Having a Child in Hospital in Northern Ireland’ in the Long Gallery in Parliament Buildings last year. My Department’s Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 aims to support and promote good mental health across the region and to provide the right help and support, at the right time, to those who need it.My Department recognises the importance of supporting those carers of sick children to look after their mental health and wellbeing. All HSC Trusts continue to offer support to carers including the supply of information, online classes, and short break opportunities to give carers some respite from their caring role. As part of the ongoing work to advance the reform of adult social care the Department has established the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The Forum’s work is being taken forward through a number of workstreams. In recognition of the particular issues and challenges faced by those with caring responsibilities, a Supporting Carers Workstream, co-chaired by Carers (NI) and my Department, has been established with a workplan focused on carers’ specific issues that relate to adult and young carers, and parent carers of sick children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he can provide funding for the Queen's University Rapid Drug Testing pilot project aiming to enhance the identification of drugs in street circulation.
answer_text: One of the main outcomes of “Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery” is to bring about a reduction in the harms from substance use. I know that the Rapid Drug Testing model was raised during the recent Private Members’ Business Motion on Addiction and Drug Related Deaths. Given the nature of the proposal, it requires consideration by partners wider than just the Department of Health. I understand preliminary discussions between the relevant partner agencies have considered this pilot proposal and that further discussions around its feasibility are planned. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available going forward. However, the current financial situation will make funding any associated proposals difficult at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is aware of the concerns of the Association of Clinical Psychologists over the regulation and registration of psychologists.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any concerns from the Association of Clinical Psychologists regarding the regulation and registration of psychologists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to strengthen the regulation and registration of psychologists in order to address concerns over the issue of unregulated mental health practitioners.
answer_text: Psychologists are regulated and registered by a UK wide regulator – the Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC). The legislation provides for nine protected titles within the Practitioner Psychologist profession. Where there is a misuse of title, with people who are not HCPC registrants practicing under a protected title, the HCPC may investigate and take any necessary action. If someone is practising under a title that is not set out in legislation, and there is no misuse or misappropriation of a protected title, the HCPC have no grounds to undertake any action. There are other professional titles in use in the wider psychological profession that do not come under statutory legislation, and some of these have voluntary registration mechanisms overseen by non-statutory bodies.The professions protected in law must be the right ones and the level of regulatory oversight proportionate to the risks to the public. In 2022, the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC), on behalf of the administrations across the UK, published the consultation document: “Healthcare Regulation: deciding when statutory regulation is appropriate”. This sought views on the criteria to be used to decide when regulation is necessary, and whether there are any unregulated professions that should be brought into statutory regulation. DHSC continues to consider the response to this consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on cross-departmental schemes with the Department of Health aimed at supporting people in prison who are struggling with mental health issues and addiction.
To ask the Minister of Health what Health and Social Care Trusts have a commissioned service for splinting for children.
answer_text: Splinting in children is carried out in a range of services in primary, secondary and community care consistent with the presenting clinical need.  Services are not specifically commissioned at procedure or specific intervention level within the HSC but form part of an episode of care across a range of clinical professions, services and specialisms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what Health and Social Care Trust areas have a commissioned service for splinting for adult patients.
answer_text: There are a range of splinting services that can be provided for patients and service users across Health and Social Care Trusts. The indication for this is based on a clinical assessment by a doctor, allied health professional (AHP) or specialist nurse.Trusts are commissioned to provide splinting services for adult patients across a range of specialties including rheumatology, trauma and orthopaedics. These services are normally provided for patients referred within the Trust’s boundaries. However, patients may be referred to regional specialties in other Trusts where they may subsequently receive splinting and patients from other Trust areas may receive splinting if these have been requested by a regional service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a patient can receive splinting services from another Health and Social Care Trust if the service is not available in their own Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are a range of splinting services that can be provided for patients and service users across Health and Social Care Trusts. The indication for this is based on a clinical assessment by a doctor, allied health professional (AHP) or specialist nurse.Trusts are commissioned to provide splinting services for adult patients across a range of specialties including rheumatology, trauma and orthopaedics. These services are normally provided for patients referred within the Trust’s boundaries. However, patients may be referred to regional specialties in other Trusts where they may subsequently receive splinting and patients from other Trust areas may receive splinting if these have been requested by a regional service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria for an adult to receive splinting services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There are a range of splinting services that can be provided for patients and service users across Health and Social Care Trusts. The indication for this is based on a clinical assessment by a doctor, allied health professional (AHP) or specialist nurse.Trusts are commissioned to provide splinting services for adult patients across a range of specialties including rheumatology, trauma and orthopaedics. These services are normally provided for patients referred within the Trust’s boundaries. However, patients may be referred to regional specialties in other Trusts where they may subsequently receive splinting and patients from other Trust areas may receive splinting if these have been requested by a regional service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of regulation in psychological therapy; and (ii) whether he intends to bring forward measures to ensure that patients are treated by therapists who are adequately qualified, trained and regulated.
answer_text: The decision on whether to regulate a profession is made by the UK Government and is informed by a number of factors. At the heart of any such decision is an assessment of the risk of harm that a profession poses to patients and the wider public.The Health and Care Professions Council (HCPC) regulates 15 health and care professions in the UK.  The job titles of psychologist, counsellor, psychotherapist and therapist are not protected by law and therefore professionals using these titles are not required to be on the HCPC register and so are not regulated. There are 9 different titles, protected by law, for practitioner psychologists which the HCPC does regulate. The UK Government and devolved administrations do not have any current plans to bring additional health or social care professions into statutory regulation, with the exception of physician associates and anaesthesia associates; nor do they intend to remove any professions that are currently subject to statutory regulation.Action 32 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 committed to a comprehensive workforce review considering existing workforce need and training, with the aim of developing a new workforce which is fit for the future. Recommendation 7 of the Mental Health Workforce Review, published in July 2023, proposes that a Psychological Therapies Framework should be developed for NI which should include a taxonomy of all Psychological Professions, defined career pathways and access routes to training programmes for existing and emerging psychological professions. This will support the adherence to best practice and delivery of high-quality therapeutic intervention from these professions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the need for a training school for child and adolescent psychotherapy; and (ii) whether he will commit to funding this training school.
answer_text: As you are aware, a Mental Health Workforce Review was published last year setting out the need to enhance the workforce resource required to deliver both Adult and Child & Adolescent Mental Health Services.  The review made recommendations regarding the future profile of the mental health workforce across all disciplines, including Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy (CAPT).		Work is ongoing to co-produce an implementation plan for the delivery of the Workforce Review recommendations. This will be the mechanism by which decisions will be made on how best to take forward the necessary enhancements to our mental health workforce, including the CAPT workforce, and associated training requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to increase commissioning for child and adolescent psychotherapy posts in the 2024-25 financial year and beyond.
answer_text: The requirements for the future mental health workforce are set out within the Mental Health Workforce Review which was completed and published last year. This review set out the need to enhance the workforce to deliver both Adult and Child & Adolescent Mental Health Services. It also set out a future mental health workforce profile across all disciplines, including Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy (CAPT). 	Work to develop a co-produced implementation plan for the Workforce Review is underway and it is through this mechanism that decisions will be made on how best to take forward the necessary enhancements to our mental health workforce, including the CAPT workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many child and adolescent psychotherapy posts he intends to commission in this current budgetary period.
answer_text: I am aware that two staff are currently undertaking training as Child and Adolescent Psychotherapists in the 2023/24 financial year. As budgets have not been agreed, I am not in a position to confirm how many child and adolescent posts will be commissioned in the 2024/25 financial year. Any such commissioning will be taken in view of wider workforce priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their strategic priorities.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department has finished its consideration of the (a) Hughes Report; and (b) Cumberledge Report; and (ii) whether he intends to introduce a redress scheme for women who have been injured by these devices.
answer_text: Hughes Report and consideration of redress optionsMy Department is considering the Hughes Report by the Patient Safety Commissioner in England including recommendations regarding options for financial and non-financial redress. My officials are linked into discussions with counterparts in England, and in Scotland and Wales, to understand emerging considerations there. Options regarding next steps for Northern Ireland will take account of these discussions, and of related timeframes which remain under consideration.Cumberledge Report My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, regular and systematic work to progress implementation of recommendations arising from the Cumberledge Report has been challenging. However, my Department has made progress on a number of recommendations. This includes:Recommendation 1: After initial consideration of the Cumberledge report and recommendations, on 8 July 2020 I apologized on behalf of the system for those patients that had been affected. I reiterated my apology in a Written Statement of 22 February 2022.Recommendation 5: In recent years, my Department has focused efforts on significant developments in relation to mesh services. For example, the Northern Ireland Mesh Centre at the Belfast City Hospital was approved by Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists in December 2017 as having the specialist expertise to manage women with mesh complications in a multi-professional team. Surgeons at the Mesh Centre, who provide the specialist clinic for mesh complications, continue to collaborate with other surgeons in the UK, developing specialist skills and expertise in the treatment of mesh complications and are now fully trained in this specialist surgery. In addition, the Belfast Trust also delivers pain and psychology clinics as part of the mesh clinic service, which are the first of their kind in the UK, and have received positive feedback from services users and patient representatives.With regard to medicines, the Department continues to work with HSC system partners to improve the safety of medicines in pregnancy more widely and ensure that sodium valproate is only prescribed where clinically appropriate. This includes communications with professionals to support new regulatory measures which came into effect on 31 January 2024 to reduce the known harms from valproate, including the significant risk of serious harm to the baby if taken during pregnancy and the risk of impaired fertility in males.The key to the provision of comprehensive treatment is through services working together with clear pathways of care supported by agreed guidelines and protocols. Recommendation 7: NHS England has launched a Medical Device Outcomes Registries programme. Each Devolved Administration can opt-in to participate in individual registries based on local assessment of need and

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the co-occurring service between mental health and addiction services within Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Both the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 and Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm and Empowering Recovery’, detail the correlation between co-occurring Mental Health and Substance Issues, and simultaneously recommend the establishment of a Dual Diagnosis Network within the Regional Single Mental Health Service for NI.The purpose of this Network is to establish better joint working between existing HSCT mental health services and substance use services in both the statutory and community and voluntary sectors.In order to help progress this issue my Department is in the process of recruiting a full-time post to develop a programme specifically relating to service pathways for people with co-occurring mental health and substance use issues. In the interim, a number of other workstreams are progressing work which will also impact on services for people with co-occurring mental health and substance use issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work to establish a Mother and Baby Mental Health Unit in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A business case for the proposed Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital site is currently being prepared. Timescales for approval of this business case and delivery of this project remain subject to capital and revenue affordability in the context of an overall challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the waiting times for the Mental Health Unit at the Belfast City Hospital; and (ii) whether this unit is operating over capacity.
answer_text: (i) to detail the waiting times for the Mental Health Unit at the Belfast City Hospital.Belfast Trust provides adult mental health inpatient facilities across two sites both in Belfast, Knockbracken Healthcare Park and Belfast City Hospital. The Mental Health Unit at the BCH (Acute Mental Health Inpatient Centre AMIC) comprises of 5 wards which cover a range of different presenting conditions for the adult population and is currently commissioned for a total of 80 beds.With regard to mental health units across Northern Ireland, the five Health & Social Care Trusts work collaboratively to support the citizens in accessing an acute inpatient mental health bed regardless of the patients home address, the system aims to offer the next available bed, to the person most in need of a bed. Due to the current sustained demand for acute mental health services, including inpatient beds, this is currently exceeding the bed capacity of the five trusts. The longest wait for an admission into AMIC in the past 12 months (February 2023 – February 2024) was 23 days.  Within the period August 2022 – August 2023 Belfast Trust have advised that the average waiting time for an adult acute admission inpatient bed was 12 hours, given the sustained pressures it is likely that this position is unchanged. (ii) whether this unit is operating over capacity.As of 12th March 2024, AMIC, was commissioned to provide 74 Acute Mental Health beds plus 6 Psychiatric Intensive Care beds (80 beds).  One bed is currently unavailable due to damage, this is being repaired, resulting in 79 available beds.  AMIC is operating over capacity due to current demand across the region, with 82 patients, some of which are using beds of patients on ‘leave’ and contingency spaces. AMIC like other acute mental health inpatient units across the region, has been and continues to, operate over the commissioned beds capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the eating disorder actions as contained within the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: Action 29 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 commits to ensure there are specialist interventions available to those who need it. 29d commits to enhance the regional eating disorder service. The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 notes provision of services for people with an eating disorder, recognising the benefits of Regional Eating Disorder Network Group (REDNG). REDNG ensures local developments are coherent and standardised as far as possible. The Strategy   acknowledges outcomes for people with an eating disorder could be improved.  In particular, evidence suggests that early intervention is key.  This means supporting services to offer specialist treatment to all those who are presenting with eating disorders, including mild to moderate cases, without delay. The Strategy highlights the need for further investment so that eating disorder services can achieve optimum staffing levels and skill mix to deliver effective care across the pathways, to include additional medical, nursing, dietetic, psychology, occupational therapy and social work staff working in the community, and providing in-reach to medical and mental health wards.  The Strategy also highlights the need for local in-patient units, medical and mental health, with in-reach and clinical consultation and the need to ensure sufficient staffing levels for the management of high-risk patients with eating disorders.  A business case is being developed in relation to Action 29 d and a number of workshops have been held with key stakeholders involved in Eating Disorder Services to shape direction and identify the case for change, based on the current service model, emerging need and setting future vision for the service.
