question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures or interventions are in place to address waiting times for autism assessments.
answer_text: My Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation. The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. Over the last six months, my staff have held meetings with all Trusts to chart the existing referral and assessment routes for paediatric ASD. After completing this mapping exercise, officials are collaborating with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Community Child Health to establish a standardised pathway across the region that aligns with NICE guidelines. The development of this pathway will incorporate best practices, emerging evidence, and the understanding that effective workforce planning is essential in meeting the demands.The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan (2023-2025) includes an action to consult on, and finalise, the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The strategy and the Framework embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of referral and diagnostic pathway. A Review of Adult Autism provision across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment. Department of Health officials are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Autism services under review, recognising that the demand for assessment and diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability and Ministerial priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the new maternity hospital will open at the Royal Victoria Hospital site.
answer_text: The New Maternity Hospital at the Royal Victoria Hospital was handed over to Belfast HSC Trust on 29 March 2024. A period of post contract work and clinical commissioning (a process to ready the new environment for operation, including inductions, familiarisation and training for staff on new systems) is now in progress, after which the building will become operational. During this period, the Trust continues to monitor the life safety and water systems. This process is expected to conclude by the end of  December 2024. However, as is the case with all major complex construction projects, until final commissioning activities are complete and signed off, programme risks remain. Further works under the contract include demolitions and construction of a link bridge between the new Maternity building and the Critical Care building. These works are currently programmed to complete in late 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are being put in place to increase staff capacity to carry out autism assessments.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his consideration of the Hughes Report on mesh implant redress payments.
answer_text: My Department continues to consider the Hughes Report by the Patient Safety Commissioner in England including the findings and recommendations in relation to financial and non-financial redress.   Officials are also linked into discussions with counterparts in England, Scotland and Wales to understand emerging considerations there.  Options regarding next steps for Northern Ireland will also take account of these discussions, and of related timeframes which remain under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the timeline to build a new footpath in Hannahstown village, West Belfast.
answer_text: Further to our meeting in October 2022 as part of my previous tenure as Infrastructure Minister I am advised that good progress has been made on the provision of a footpath in Hannahstown village.  I understand that negotiations with landowners on accommodation works are expected to commence this month.  Subject to completion of these negotiations, a detailed design of the scheme is scheduled to be completed in May 2024.Construction is expected to commence in the 2025/26 financial year subject to successful land acquisition and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to increase commissioning for speech and language therapist undergraduate places in 2024-25 from 28 to 40, in line with the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029.
answer_text: The commissioning of pre-registration education and training by my Department is reviewed annually, taking into account a range of factors including staffing and vacancy levels, recommendations from workforce reviews, priorities, and affordability.  I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, which will require increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40.A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce. However, I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the recent review into residential rehabilitation services for addiction.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for the Department of Health, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental actions and support.Outcome C of the Substance Use Strategy is that “People have Access to High Quality Treatment and Support Services.”  To contribute to and deliver this Outcome, a ‘Review of Regional Tier 4a and Tier 4b Addiction Services in Northern Ireland’ was commissioned by the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) in 2023. A review team was appointed in early 2023 and the Review was carried out between March 2023 and February 2024. The initial draft report is now being reviewed and will be presented to SPPG’s Senior Management Team for approval. In carrying out the Review the review team considered the following:-	An objective analysis of existing services across Tier 4 Addiction Services with consideration of other tiers and current pathways through direct observation and analysis of activity and performance data.-	Scoping of current infrastructure and capacity within current inpatient (Tier 4a) detoxification services including thresholds for admission, clinical and care outcomes, service user and carer involvement and experience and staffing levels.-	Scoping of current infrastructure and capacity within residential (Tier 4b) rehabilitation services including thresholds for admission, clinical and care outcomes, service user and care involvement and experience and staffing levels.-	Consideration of other factors including physical environment, commissioning and training.-	Exploration of in-patient detoxification and residential rehabilitation services to understand demand, patient flow, dovetailing between Tier 4a and 4b practice models, procedures/systems, challenges across Trusts.-	Analysis of service user profile and associated clinical outcomes across detoxification and residential rehabilitation services.-	Gap analysis, identifying requirements for a best evidence regional service model across the Trusts.-	Consideration of best practice and service delivery within Tier 4 services in assessment, treatment and care outside of N. Ireland.-	Consideration of measures to reduce variation in practice, admissions, treatment, discharge arrangements, professional roles and delivery of care pathways-	Scoping of best practice models of Tier 4 In-patient and residential rehabilitation for N. Ireland to achieve best outcomes, processes and standards.The recommendations within the Review will be considered carefully in line with the NI Executive’s Substance Use Strategy, and will likely be subject to a formal public consultation during 2024. This engagement will further inform an implementation plan that will be established to deliver the recommendations and ensure people acr

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration has been given to reintroducing the Happy Healthy Minds pilot project.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023. An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners. The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Jack's Promise campaign and petition that was previously presented to the Assembly, what consideration he is giving to the establishment of a residential rehabilitation facility in West Belfast.
answer_text: I am aware of Jack’s Promise through the public petition laid before the Assembly in November 2021. The number of signatories clearly demonstrated the depth of feeling on this issue. Addressing the harm related to substances is a key priority for my Department, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy ‘Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery’. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental actions and support.To address a key action within the strategy, I have tasked the Public Health Agency and DoH Strategic Planning & Performance Group to co-produce, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan, to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework. Following recent public consultation, the draft Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan is now being reviewed for alignment with the work of the Regional Mental Health Service and the Integrated Care System. Two other priority pieces of work were conducted in tandem with co-developing the new delivery framework and these are due to report shortly – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland.The recommendations within the Tier 4 Review will be considered carefully in line with Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery, and will likely be subject to a formal public consultation during 2024. This engagement will further inform an implementation plan that will be established to deliver the recommendations and ensure people across Northern Ireland have equal access to community detox services for alcohol and other drugs.It is anticipated that any decision to commission residential rehabilitation services in the future will be informed by the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan along with the Tier 4 Review, and will be made in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide information on the new Public Health strategy that is replacing the Making Life Better Strategy 2012 – 2023.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the overarching strategic framework for public health through which the Executive committed to creating the conditions for individuals, families, and communities to take greater control over their lives, and be enabled and supported to lead healthy lives. MLB is underpinned by a range of public health strategies and policies which focus on specific issues, including substance use, suicide prevention, tobacco use and obesity prevention. A mid-term review of Making Life Better was underway in 2020, however this work had to be paused to enable the Department of Health to divert very significant staff resource into responding to the COVID-19 pandemic. It is important to note however that work to implement Making Life Better, delivering on its underpinning public health strategies, continued.The Department remains under significant resourcing pressure as we emerge from the pandemic and recover. A full review of Making Life Better will recommence in the near future. The existing framework will remain in place until this work is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering bringing forward legislation for the office of the Mental Health Champion in the future.
answer_text: A number of options were considered in the appointment of a Mental Health Champion (MHC) for Northern Ireland, which included the establishment of a MHC with legislative powers.  It was agreed by the Executive to create the role without legislative powers, subject to review should it become apparent that the role is not working without dedicated legislation.  At present, there is no intention to bring forward legislation for the office of the MHC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will to take to address the postcode lottery in relation to counselling provision.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 contains a number of actions which, individually and collectively, are designed to help ensure that individuals have good and equitable access to mental health services.Adult mental health services in Northern Ireland are delivered in line with the mental health care pathway and the stepped care model. In general, steps one and two are provided through primary care. This includes counselling, involvement of the community and voluntary sectors and, where available, referral to talking therapies/ wellbeing/ psychological therapies hubs.  Many GPs have signed up to a Local Enhanced Service to provide a mild to moderate depression counselling service in-house.  My Department has been working to make additional mental health support available in primary care through the Multi Disciplinary Team programme, which has introduced new social work, physiotherapy and mental health roles into general practice. Moving forward, the Strategy commits to changing how mental health services are structured, with a greater focus on the community.  This means reorganising mental health services around the community, with an increased focus on our GPs. This will involve increasing the availability of therapy hubs to meet local needs and will ensure a focus is maintained on, and will result in improved outcomes for, individuals. On 10 October, I announced the new arrangements for a Regional Mental Health Service operating across the five HSC Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development. The objective of the RMHS is to provide people across Northern Ireland with equitable access to high quality, regionally consistent but locally based mental health services, working across primary and secondary mental healthcare and with the full integration of the community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether the level of data collection within the health service does not allow for scrutiny of how effectively public money is being spent.
answer_text: There are significant volumes and types of data collected and assessed within the HSC and an ongoing drive to ensure that this data is continually refined and utilised to best effect.  The data currently available is used to assess how effectively public money is being spent in the following ways:Baseline resources, such as those committed to deliver services in Trusts, are assessed through activity and outcomes performance monitoring at service level. This is supported by financial performance data seeking to ensure that services move towards the most efficient delivery model at the lowest possible cost; and  New investment options are considered as part of business case processes regarding efficiency and effectiveness, such decisions will consider a variety of data sources both within and external to the HSC.Further digitisation, integration and assessment of significant volumes of data from a wide range of sources remains core to the efficiency drive within the HSC and my Department is investing in information systems, such as the Encompass programme, to ensure that focus is brought back to these areas as the services rebuild post Covid-19.  This is an important and ongoing strand of the efficiency agenda for the DoH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will address the lack of inspection and regulation of mental health services.
answer_text: The Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 (“the 2003 Order”) constitutes the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) and provides general duties and responsibilities of the organisation. The 2003 Order does not provide specific duties in respect of mental health services.The duties of RQIA in relation to Mental Health are set out in Article 86 of the Mental Health (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. My Department is aware of the changing health and social care environment and has carried out a fundamental review of health and social care regulatory framework.  My officials have developed a new draft regulatory policy that includes the principles of regulation, along with the broad scope of services to be regulated and the proposal that the regulator should have wider powers of enforcement etc.   This work has been the first phase of the process before moving on to phase 2. As part of my Department’s response to the pandemic the launch of the consultation on phase 1 was delayed.  Before a draft policy is consulted upon it will be subject to further review taking into account any additional lessons from the pandemic and any other regulatory and quality issues that have emerged over the past few years.It is my intention that a Review of Regulation will be taken forward during the current mandate of the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will address the lack of data collection for waiting times of those seeking mental health provision.
answer_text: Data on waiting times is retrieved by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) from the Trusts on a monthly basis.  This data contains Mental Health waiting times broken down by Service Teams and includes breach analysis on Mental Health Outpatient waiting times.As part of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31, work is ongoing to establish a regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework.  This will involve collecting data on quantitative metrics such as the number of referrals, waiting time to first appointment etc, alongside qualitative data and outcomes measurement across mental health services. These metrics are core elements of an outcomes framework that will play a key role in monitoring the quality and safety of services and in helping ensure equitable access to services across the region.It is anticipated that implementation of the Outcomes Framework will be aligned to delivery of the Encompass project to ensure that the required mental health data can be captured in a timely and effective manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to carry out a rapid review of counselling provision across the five Trusts to include (i) counselling in GP practices; (ii) the number of individuals referred; (iii) the numbers accessing therapy; (iv) the numbers on waiting lists; (v) the duration of waiting time; (vi) the number of sessions being provided; (vii) therapy-based outcomes; and (viii) the availability of qualified counsellors for immediate deployment.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many GPs are trained in suicide prevention; (ii) how many doctors are trained in suicide prevention; (iii) how many nurses are trained in suicide prevention; and (iv) what organisation is responsible for recording this data.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally and suicide prevention training data held by HSC Trusts cannot be broken down by profession. HSC Trusts have responsibility around staff training and how they collect that data. GPs are largely responsible for directing their own Continuing Professional Development which should cover the whole of their practice and is reviewed annually as part of their annual appraisal.Suicide prevention and emotional health and wellbeing training will continue to be widely promoted and staff encouraged to undertake training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will develop a separate mental health data strategy to sit alongside the Mental Health Strategy. as recommended by the UK Statistics Authority.
answer_text: The Review of Mental Health Statistics in Northern Ireland report by the UK Statistics Authority, which was published in September 2021, made a number of recommendations to improve the gathering of mental health statistics in Northern Ireland. These recommendations were made in the context of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 which recognises that, in order to ensure we have the right services to meet the needs of the population, we must have data to measure outcomes. Action 34 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2023 seeks to develop a regional Outcomes Framework in collaboration with service users and professionals, to underpin and drive service development and delivery.Considerable work has been progressed to develop the regional Mental Health Outcomes Framework and it is expected that, once agreed, implementation of the framework will commence, aligned with the development of the Encompass project to ensure that the required mental health data can be captured in a timely and effective manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the regulatory oversight and legislation governing community addiction rehabilitation services provided by (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) the community and voluntary sector.

To ask the Minister of Health how information relating to (i) referrals; (ii) the number of sessions; (iii) missed appointments; and (iv) waiting times, for mental health practitioners in primary care multidisciplinary teams will be collected, and whether there are plans to regularly report this information.
answer_text: (i) ReferralsThe number of referrals to MDT Mental Health Practitioners is recorded on GP Clinical Systems and collated monthly at Federation level via SPPG GPIP systems.This data will be reported quarterly to the MDT Steering Group.(ii) The number of sessionsNo data on the number of sessions is collated The number of consultations with MDT Mental Health Practitioners is recorded on GP Clinical Systems and collated monthly at Federation level via SPPG GPIP systems.In line with MDT Model Parameters approximately 85% of Mental Health Practitioner time is Practice based and 15% is Fieldwork based.This data will be reported quarterly to the MDT Steering Group.(iii) Missed appointmentsNo data currently collated by SPPG, but may be held locally within GP Practices.(iv) Waiting timesThe MH Teams do not carry caseloads or waiting lists to access these practice based services, with patients scheduled at point of contact within the GP Practice. SPPG is currently working with GP Federations to ascertain average waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when AQW 3158/22-27 will be answered.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of the European Social Fund (ESF), and indeed the significant concerns raised by voluntary and community groups, including mental health and disability groups, about the potential impact on the provision of their services. It is important that there is clarity on the arrangements post-ESF as soon as possible - including the role of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund as its replacement. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on the replacement of EU Funding.  DoF continues to represent all departments, including Health, in engagement with Whitehall, to try to ensure that the UK Shared Prosperity Fund will cover gaps that will be created when the European Social Fund support endsUnfortunately, the very challenging funding position and budget uncertainty, means that I am not in a position to confirm that the Department can support those services previously funded by the European Social Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on (i) when he will bring forward a strategy for menopause services and supports needed in Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) what discussions he has had with his officials on a regional menopause policy.
answer_text: I recognise that menopause affects women across all sectors of society, and if the Northern Ireland Executive is supportive of cross-Departmental work in this area, I would expect that my Department would, of course, contribute to this. In recent months I have been participating in the UK Menopause Taskforce, meeting with Ministerial counterparts and colleagues from the other UK jurisdictions, as well as experts within the menopause field. The Taskforce was established to strengthen coordination and raise awareness of the menopause and its impact across the UK, under the shared aim of improving care and support for women experiencing the menopause across all sectors of society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timeframe for the opening of the new maternity hospital.
answer_text: The building contractor is due to hand over the New Maternity Hospital in January 2023. Following handover Belfast Trust have a 20 week period to operationalise Maternity and Neo-natal Services in the new Maternity Hospital. It is expected that the building will be fully operational by June 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting list for appointments with a menopause specialist in each Health and Social Care Trust, broken down by length of waiting time.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) funding provided; (ii) organisation responsible; and (iii) allocated Departmental staff, to campaigns to address menopause.
answer_text: The public information campaigns function within the Public Health Agency (PHA) is responsible for the overall development, management, implementation and monitoring of multi-media campaigns, which cover a wide range of health issues and programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the contract has been awarded for the School Enhancement Programme work at St John the Baptist Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: The tender process for the appointment of a Design Team for the School Enhancement Programme project at St John the Baptist Primary School has completed. The award letter issued to the design team, W.H. Stephens, on 2nd September 2022 and the school was notified of the award of contract on the same day.An introductory meeting has been organised for the Department and W. H. Stephens on 12th October 2022, after which the first Project Board will be arranged with the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many respite facilities are open to families or carers in the West Belfast Constituency; and (ii) the location and services available at each facility.
answer_text: Service users with a learning disability can access respite facilities within the Belfast locality/Trust, in accordance with their assessed need. Specifically within West Belfast service users who are known to the Learning Disability Programme of Care, their families or carers can avail of respite facilities at Trench Park, which is based in West Belfast – BT11 area.  There are 2 beds available in this facility and it has the necessary facilities to meet the needs of service users.  E.g. Tracking hoists.  For service users who are known to the Physical and Sensory Disability programme of care there are no beds within the West Belfast locality for the specific purpose of respite.  If respite is required the Trust would seek to explore admission to a Nursing / Residential home in the locality dependent on the service user’s needs.Day care provision is also available for service users and this can be considered as a form of respite for families and carers.  Beechall Day Centre in Andersonstown is specifically tailored to meet the needs of people with physical and sensory disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline what solutions there are to the European Social Fund funding shortages being faced by mental health and disability groups; and (ii) to confirm that groups in receipt of this funding will be supported by the Department to keep their services open.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of the European Social Fund (ESF), and indeed the significant concerns raised by voluntary and community groups, including mental health and disability groups, about the potential impact on the provision of their services. It is important that there is clarity on the arrangements post-ESF as soon as possible - including the role of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund as its replacement. The Department of Finance (DoF) leads on the replacement of EU Funding.  DoF continues to represent all departments, including Health, in engagement with Whitehall, to try to ensure that the UK Shared Prosperity Fund will cover gaps that will be created when the European Social Fund support endsUnfortunately, the very challenging funding position and budget uncertainty, means that I am not in a position to confirm that the Department can support those services previously funded by the European Social Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the review of mental health crisis services, including (i) how many of the 15 recommendations in the review report have been delivered; (ii) the funding spent on delivery of the recommendations; and (iii) the funding that has been ring-fenced to help deliver the recommendations.
answer_text: The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and Public Health Agency (PHA) are leading on the implementation of the new crisis service through the 10 actions in the policy, with HSC Trusts responsible for the implementation and delivery of regionally consistent mental health crisis services.  The update provided to my Department earlier in the year stated that out of the 31 actions scheduled for completion by 31 March 2022, 9 have been marked complete, with the remaining 22 actions being taken forward into Year 1. Progress to date has primarily been impacted by PHA staff being redeployed to Covid duties.The Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Implementation Plan highlights that some actions will be initiated without the need for additional funds, and that those actions which require additional funding will be considered through the development of appropriate business cases.The Funding Plan published alongside the Mental Health Strategy 2021-31 estimated that the revenue funding required to deliver the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service ranges between £2.932m and £8.024m per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's plans for the future of out-of-hours GP services.
answer_text: The Out of Hours service is under considerable strain and I am aware of the continuing pressures faced by the service across Northern Ireland.  My Department has established a working group, in line with the recommendation from the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority review of Out of Hours services, to develop a new model for the service.  The working group membership comprises representatives of HSC Trusts and other Out of Hours providers, GP representatives and officials of my Department.The working group is cognisant of the work ongoing within my Department regards wider unscheduled and emergency care structure and the implementation of No More Silos.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the recommendations regarding mesh implants in the First Do No Harm report, published in July 2020.
answer_text: Following my Written Statement to the Assembly on 22 February 2022, work has continued to implement the ‘First Do No Harm’ Report recommendations, including in relation to mesh implants.With regard to recommendation 5 and a specialist centre, a dedicated Mesh Clinic has been established in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust City Hospital since March 2019. This clinic has been set up to address the ongoing care of those patients suffering complications from vaginal mesh procedures. The delivery of this service was impacted by the pandemic. However, mesh outpatient and scanning clinics resumed on 17th June 2021. Surgery is planned to resume on 30th July 2022.  A Consultant Clinical Psychologist has also been recruited and a pain management programme will commence in August 2022.With regard to recommendation 7, a central patient identifiable implant database linked to specifically created registries, my officials are continuing to engage with colleagues in NHS Digital to enable Northern Ireland participation in the NHS Digital Pelvic Floor Registry. This registry is available to hold patient identifiable data and medical device implant details along with  a clinical registry for surveillance and outcomes.  It is intended that a trial of the necessary electronic data collection and submission to the registry will be undertaken by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in the near future. Whilst not limited to the specific recommendations on mesh, you may also be interested to know that work is progressing in respect of recommendation 8, transparency of payments made to clinicians. My officials have been working with colleagues across the UK to develop a proposed approach for the reporting and publishing of clinicians’ interests that may be used across all healthcare sectors in the four nations. Officials are also making progress on work, which is being led by the Department of Health and Social Care, to develop policy proposals on the mandatory reporting by industry of payments made to clinicians.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to progress the new co-occurring mental health and addiction service as outlined in the Mental Health Strategy.
answer_text: The treatment of co-occurring mental health and substance use problems, frequently called Dual Diagnosis, is an issue that was raised prominently during the development of both the new Substance Use and Mental Health Strategies.  Many responses from stakeholders highlighted the need to develop a more holistic treatment system to provide patient-centred care structured around the needs of individual service users.  This is why an action was included within the new Mental Health Strategy to create a Managed Care Network for Dual Diagnosis. This has similarly been highlighted under the priorities section of the new Substance Use Strategy.The aim is that those with mental health and addiction issues will get the best care and treatment available, and that close working, training and a Managed Care Network will provide the optimum route for embedding a “no wrong door” approach.  Work is now underway on the implementation of the two strategies.  However, full implementation is dependent on the provision of additional funding.  Under the Substance Use Strategy, a specific group has been established to look at co-occurring mental and substance use problems and to set out what other actions will be required to address this issue.In July, I published the Mental Health Strategy Delivery Plan for 2022/23, which was developed to outline the implementation work that can be taken forward within existing resources and to ensure that full implementation of other priority actions contained therein can commence, should additional funding be made available during 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what the annual spend is on (i) cancer services; and (ii) cancer research.
answer_text: Unfortunately, it not possible for me to provide an actual figure for annual spend on cancer services as cancer care pathways are not stand-alone (for example, we do not have separate cancer capacity within outpatients, imaging, pathology, surgery etc). Therefore, the vast majority of cancer care is embedded within wider elective and unscheduled primary, secondary and tertiary care provision. The investment in cancer research from the Health and Social Care Research and Development (HSC R&D) Fund for the most recent financial year (2021-22) was £2.6 million.  Around £1.5 million of this supported infrastructure (Northern Ireland Cancer Trials Network and NI Biobank staff), which are ongoing costs, and the remainder was spread across a number of funded awards, which are time-bounded and completely or partially focused on cancer research.I can advise that the proportion of the HSC R&D Fund spent on cancer research in Northern Ireland is in line with that of other UK funders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what counselling provision is available in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary schools in West Belfast.
answer_text: Healthy Happy Minds, a pilot counselling and therapeutic support programme, has been introduced in all primary schools as one of a range measures supporting the implementation of the ‘The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ which was published in February 2021. The pilot was initially due to conclude at the end of March 2022 but was extended until June 2022. I can now confirm that the pilot will continue until the end of December 2022.An Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) has been available to all DE grant aided post primary schools since September 2007 and to post-primary age pupils in special schools since January 2011. The aim of ICSS is to ensure that all post primary aged pupils have access to a consistent, high quality, professional and independent counselling service and to reduce under achievement and improve the life chances of young people by enhancing their educational development and fostering their health, wellbeing and social inclusion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on work to progress a new regional mental health service.
answer_text: Action 31 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 commits to the development of a regional Mental Health Service, operating across the five HSC Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development.There has been considerable engagement to date to develop proposals for the Regional Mental Health Service, including ensuring that it fits within the Integrated Care System model currently under development.  A Review Report, setting out proposals for the Regional Mental Health Service is nearing completion and, subject to necessary approvals, it is anticipated that an implementation plan for delivery of the regional service will be developed in the coming months.  However, it should be noted that full implementation of the Mental Health Strategy, including the establishment of the Regional Mental Health Service, will be subject to the provision of additional funding to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria for accessing (i) inpatient; and (ii) outpatient and community addiction services, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The referral criteria applied by healthcare practitioners in each Health and Social Care Trust for access to (i) Tier 4a Inpatient treatment and (ii) outpatient and community addiction services, is delivered in line with the UK Clinical Guidelines for the Clinical Management of Drug Misuse and Dependence and based upon NICE guidance CG 52 & CG 115.To gain access to Tier 4 in-patient services, a full bio-psycho-social assessment is undertaken by a key worker when a patient is referred into Trust-based Community Addictions Teams. Staff from local Community Addictions Teams explain all of the potential care options available. Working in partnership with service users, where appropriate, they will arrange admission to the unit best suited to the individual’s clinical care needs. For a patient to be considered for inpatient medical treatment in a Tier 4 unit, there must be an identified goal regarding their substance use and it is usually expected that the patient shows a commitment to this change by attending and working with their key worker prior to admission to a unit. The patient must have the willingness to work with other patients in group settings. For patients who have particularly acute unstable mental health, admission to an addiction inpatient unit may not be appropriate until their underlying condition stabilises due to the fact they may not be able to fully participate in group and rehabilitation work.Outpatient and/or Community Addiction Services are accessed by individuals who are experiencing issues with harmful use of substances or substance dependency, and with an expressed desire to moderate or cease their substance intake. All referrals to these services are managed in a fair and equitable way in line with clinical guidance as detailed above.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what progress has been made on a new maternity strategy and when this strategy will be published.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for gynaecology services in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Outpatient and inpatient waiting times are published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  These publications refer to the position at the end of a given quarter, detailing how many patients are waiting for a first consultant led outpatient appointment or inpatient or day case admission broken down by HSC Trust, Specialty and length of time waiting. The most recent quarterly National Statistics publications of Waiting Times for the position at 31st March 2022 are available at the following links:Outpatients - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-outpatient-waiting-times-march-2022.Inpatients - https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-inpatient-and-day-case-waiting-times-march-2022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to provide a breakdown of the funding for each multi-disciplinary team; and (ii) whether this funding is based on need.
To ask the Minister of Health whether multi-disciplinary teams who are working in areas of high deprivation are entitled to additional funding to support a greater level of need.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when the School Enhancement Programme work will commence at St John the Baptist Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: The tender process for the appointment of a Design Team for the School Enhancement Programme project at St John the Baptist Primary School is underway, with tenders due to be returned by 26 July 2022. Following assessment of the tenders, the contract will be awarded. A Project Board will be established following the appointment of a Design Team. This will enable the school and relevant stakeholders to have input into the project and oversight of the programme. It will be the responsibility of the Design Team to prepare a programme of work for the project once they have been appointed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the business case for a perinatal mental health inpatient unit, including the length of time spent to date developing the business case.
answer_text: Implementation of the specialist perinatal mental health services is being implemented on a phased approach, with phase one focussing on the roll out of community perinatal mental health teams.  In parallel, preliminary scoping work has also commenced on the establishment of a Mother and Baby Unit for the region, including ongoing engagement with Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a working group has been established to look at mitigations for Health and Social Care pensions issues affecting staff.
answer_text: The Department is in ongoing discussions with Trusts and member representatives regarding pensions issues.  The Department continues to monitor the current situation and developments across the UK.
